'Sit down. Tell me about the woman in Palanthas. Tell me all of it. Every year since.' Sit beside him against the oak and listen as the fire burns down and the story comes out, not in order, not cleanly, but in the fragments a broken man offers when he has decided to let someone see the pieces. It takes hours. He cries once — silently, briefly, furious about it. You do not comment. When he finishes, the sky is grey with predawn and he looks lighter. Not healed. But lighter. 'No one has ever listened to the whole thing,' he says. 'Thank you.' The word sounds unfamiliar in his mouth. He is learning it

DATE_MIRA.title: THE HEALER'S HANDS
prose: She has not stopped moving since the column halted. You have been watching — not deliberately, but with the peripheral awareness that comes from caring about someone who does not care about themselves with sufficient diligence. She checked Aldric's shoulder. She rebound Sera's wrist. She counted the supplies twice and reorganised the surgical kit three times and now she is sitting with her back against the supply cart and her hands in her lap and she is not moving because she has run out of motion.
'Mira.'

'I am fine.' She says it the way she says everything — practically, efficiently, with the specific conviction of someone who has diagnosed herself and refuses to accept the diagnosis. Then: 'I am not fine. I have been fine for eleven hours and the contract has expired.'

You sit beside her. Close enough that your shoulders touch. She does not lean away. She leans in — just barely, a degree of weight that says she has been holding herself upright through professional obligation and the obligation has clocked out for the night.

Her hands are shaking. Not dramatically — a fine tremor in the fingers, the kind that comes from sustained precision work. Healing hands. They have been healing all day and now they have nothing to heal and they do not know how to stop.

You take her hands. She lets you. They are cold and rougher than you expected — callused differently from sword hands, the pads thick from grinding herbs, a thin scar across the left palm where she told you a blade slipped during her first field surgery. You hold them and she watches you hold them with the expression of someone who has spent her life putting other people back together and has forgotten what it feels like from the other side.

'No one—' She stops. Starts again. 'I am always the one who holds the hands. It is what I do. I hold the hands and I count the breathing and I am steady because someone has to be steady. And it works. It always works. Except that after a while you forget that your hands need holding too.'

She is crying. Quietly, efficiently, even her grief organised — tears running but breathing controlled, the discipline of a woman who learned a long time ago to fall apart without making a sound. You do not wipe the tears away. You hold her hands and you let her cry and you are steady because someone has to be steady and tonight it is your turn.

After a while the trembling stops. She breathes out. Looks at your hands holding hers. 'You have a blister forming on your left index finger,' she says. 'The grip on your sword is too tight. I will wrap it in the morning.' She laughs — small, wet, the ghost of humour returning. 'I cannot stop. I am sorry. I look at you and I see things that need tending.'
'Then tend them.' You turn your hand over. Offer her the blister, the calluses, the accumulated small damages of a month on the road. Not because they need healing. Because she needs to heal.

She unwraps a strip of bandage — her hands steady now, the muscle memory taking over. She wraps your finger with the absent-minded care of someone performing a task so familiar it has become a form of love. When she finishes, she does not let go.
'Stay,' she says. 'I do not need healing. I need—' She looks at you. 'I need someone to sit with me and not need anything.'

choices.text: Stay. All night. She falls asleep against your shoulder sometime around the third hour and you do not move because the weight of her sleeping is the most important thing you have ever carried. When she wakes she is embarrassed. You are not

Kiss the top of her head. 'I am here. I do not need anything.' She makes a sound that is either a laugh or a sob or both and her arms go around you and she holds on the way she never allows herself to hold on — not as a healer, not as the steady one, just as a woman who is frightened and tired and has finally found the one person she trusts enough to be both

'Tell me about the scar on your left hand. The real story, not the field surgery version.' She tells you. It is a longer story than you expected and it involves a boy she loved when she was sixteen and a window she climbed out of and a knife she grabbed the wrong way in the dark. She is laughing by the end. Properly laughing. The tremor is gone

DATE_SERA.title: AFTER THE ASSESSMENT
prose: She is on the ridge because Sera is always on the ridge. High ground, clear sightlines, two exits — she chooses her positions the way she chooses everything: with the absolute precision of someone who has turned survival into an art form. You climb up. She watched you coming for two minutes before you started climbing and she already has an opinion about your approach angle.
'Your left,' she says. 'You favour your left on uneven ground. An archer would notice.'
'I am not worried about archers. I am worried about you.'

Something shifts in her face. The professional mask — the Scout Nighthollow mask, precise and cool and always slightly ahead of everyone else — holds for a half-second longer and then adjusts. Not gone. Loosened. The way armour shifts when you undo one strap.

She looks at you. The moonlight does something to her face that the daylight does not — softens the angles, finds the version of her that exists underneath the competence. She is beautiful in the way that dangerous things are beautiful, which is to say: you are very aware that this is a person who could kill you from this distance before you registered the shot, and the fact that she is choosing not to is its own form of intimacy.

'I do not do this,' she says. 'I observe. I report. I do not—' She gestures between you. The gesture is the least precise thing she has ever done. 'This. Whatever this is. I do not have a field manual for this.'
'You have assessed me.'

'Constantly.' No hesitation. 'Your sword grip, your footwork, your breathing pattern during stress, the way your eyes move when you enter a room, the scar on your right forearm that you got from a training accident you have never told anyone about.' She is not looking at you. She is looking at the horizon. 'I have assessed you every day since the courtyard. I have filed detailed mental reports. None of them are adequate.'
'What are they missing?'

She turns. Steps closer. The distance between you narrows to an arm's length and then to less and she moves the way she moves through the dark — without sound, without warning, with the absolute commitment of someone who has chosen her angle of approach and is not going to change it.

'They are missing the part where I stop assessing.' Her voice is lower than you have heard it. Her hand finds the front of your shirt — not pulling, just resting there, feeling the heartbeat underneath. 'Your pulse just increased. You are breathing faster. Your pupils are dilated.' She is cataloguing you and the cataloguing has become something else, the clinical attention turning warm and focused and very close. 'I am going to stop assessing now.'

She kisses you. It is — like everything Sera does — precise. Deliberate. And then approximately three seconds in, it is not precise at all. It is the controlled professional losing control and the loss is dizzying and mutual and her hands are in your hair and your hands are on her waist and she makes a sound against your mouth that is the least tactical thing she has ever done.
When she pulls back her breathing is uneven. She looks at you. The mask is gone entirely. Underneath it she is younger and more startled and more wanting than you have ever seen her.
'I am going to need a moment,' she says, 'to recalibrate.'
'Take your time.'
'That is not what I want to do with my time.' She pulls you back.

choices.text: The ridge. The moonlight. The woman who sees everything, finally letting herself be seen. Stay until you have both forgotten what precision feels like. The night is very long and neither of you uses it for sleeping and in the morning she will be Sera Nighthollow again, the mask back in place, the assessment running. But she will look at you once, across the camp, and the mask will slip for a half-second, and that half-second will be worth everything

'I see you too.' Tell her what you have observed — the way she checks the perimeter twice when she is nervous, the way she touches the bowstring when she is thinking, the way she looks south and her jaw tightens. Show her that assessment goes both ways. She is very quiet when you finish. Then: 'No one has ever watched me back.' The second kiss is slower. The third is not slow at all

Hold her. Not strategically. Not with any angle of approach. Just hold her and let the competence rest for one night. She is stiff at first — her body does not know how to be held without a tactical purpose — and then something releases, a tension she has been carrying since long before you met her, and she fits against you with a sigh that says she has been waiting for someone to make this feel safe

DATE_TARIEL.title: THE OBSERVER OBSERVED
prose: Her notebook is closed. You have never seen Tariel's notebook closed when she is awake. She is sitting at the edge of the firelight with her knees drawn up and the closed notebook in her lap and she is looking at the fire with the expression of someone who has run out of observations. Not because there is nothing to observe — because she has observed too much and the weight of it has exceeded the notebook's capacity to contain it.
She hears you coming. She hears everything. But she does not open the notebook.

'I have been trying to write about you,' she says. No preamble. Tariel does not do preamble. 'For seven weeks I have been trying to write about you and I cannot find the right framework. You are not the Order. You are not the system. You defy categorisation and I find it—' She pauses. Chooses the next word with the care of someone who knows words are the most dangerous things she carries. '—distracting.'

You sit beside her. The fire crackles. She watches you sit down with the attentiveness she usually reserves for institutions, the dark eyes cataloguing the angle of your shoulders, the distance you chose, the way you breathe.

'I came here to study the knighthood,' she says. 'To understand why people choose this. The Oath, the structure, the willingness to die for a system. I have catalogued every mechanism of indoctrination. I have identified every leverage point.' She turns to you. The firelight catches the silver at her temples — she is not old, but she has earned those strands. 'And then you happened. And you are the one thing I cannot explain with leverage points.'
'Maybe it is not about leverage.'

'That is what I am afraid of.' Her voice drops. The professional distance — the journalist's distance, the observer's distance — is still there, but thin now, translucent, and you can see through it to the woman underneath who has spent her career at arm's length from everything and has discovered, at the worst possible time, that arm's length is too far.

She puts the notebook on the ground. The gesture is deliberate. She does not put the notebook down. The notebook is her armour. Without it she is just a woman at a fire, looking at you, and the vulnerability of it is breathtaking.
'I am going to say something that I cannot take back,' she says. 'And I need you to understand that I have never said it to anyone and I am not certain I know how.'
'Say it.'

'I am not studying you anymore. I stopped studying you weeks ago. What I have been doing is—' She looks at the fire. Back at you. 'What I have been doing is looking at you and feeling something that I do not have a framework for and I am terrified. Not of the war. Of this. Of you. Of what it means that I would rather be here — without the notebook, without the distance, without any of the things that keep me safe — than anywhere else in Krynn.'

choices.text: 'Then be here.' Take the notebook. Set it further away. Move closer. 'No framework. No distance. Just here.' She is trembling. Not from cold. You brush the hair from her face and she closes her eyes and when you kiss her it is the quietest, most careful kiss — not because you are uncertain, but because this woman has never let anyone this close and the gentleness is a form of respect. She kisses back. Slowly. Then not slowly. Then the notebook is forgotten and the fire is forgotten and she is holding onto you like the last true thing in a world full of analysis

'Write about this. Right now. Not the analysis. The feeling. What it actually feels like.' She stares at you. Opens the notebook. Writes. Her hand is shaking. When she finishes, she reads it aloud — halting, raw, nothing like her dispatches, everything like the truth. When she looks up there are tears on her face and she is smiling and the smile is the most unguarded thing you have ever seen from Tariel Duskmantle

Say nothing. Take her hand. Sit with her in the quiet space where observation ends and presence begins. She laces her fingers through yours. Holds on. The fire burns down and the stars come out and she leans against you and for the first time since you have known her, Tariel Duskmantle is not watching. She is just here

DATE_THESSALY.title: THE THIRD VERSION
prose: She is sitting on the fallen log at the edge of camp with her notebook open and her pen behind her ear and the locket in her hand. The locket is open. You have never seen the locket open when she knows someone is watching.
She does not close it when she sees you. That is how you know tonight is different.
'Sit,' she says. Not a request. An observation that you were going to sit anyway and she is acknowledging the inevitability.

You sit. The notebook is between you. She has written three columns — you can see the headers even in the firelight: PROFESSIONAL, HONEST, and a third column she has scratched out so thoroughly the paper is torn.

'The third column,' she says, watching you look at it, 'is the one where I write what I actually want instead of what I am willing to admit.' She turns toward you. The firelight catches the locket and the thing inside it — a scrap of paper, handwriting you cannot read at this distance. 'I am tired of scratching things out.'

She reaches up and takes the pen from behind her ear. Holds it out to you. 'Write something in the third column. Not what you think I want to read. What you would write if you knew no one would ever see it.'

You take the pen. Your fingers brush hers. She does not pull away. Her hand is smaller than you expected — ink-stained at the fingertips, a callus on the middle finger from years of writing, and warm in a way that makes you aware of how cold the night air is by contrast.

You write something. She reads it. Her face does the thing it does when she has received information that changes a conclusion she was already committed to — a small reorganisation behind the eyes, a breath held and released.
'Good,' she says. Very quiet. Then she takes the pen back and writes something beneath it. She turns the notebook so you can read it.
It is not professional. It is not even honest, in the way she uses the word — careful, verified, sourceable. It is the other thing. The thing she does not put in dispatches.
She watches you read it. 'I would like,' she says, 'to stop being the person who writes about the world happening and start being the person it happens to. Tonight. With you.'

The locket is still open. In the firelight you can finally read the scrap of paper inside it: a single line in her own handwriting, old enough that the ink has faded. *Remember what wanting feels like.*
She is remembering.

choices.text: Kiss her. She has written enough. The pen falls somewhere. Neither of you looks for it. The notebook slides off the log. Her hand is on the back of your neck and she kisses the way she writes — precisely, thoroughly, with the focused attention of someone who has been composing this sentence for weeks and is finally putting the pen to the page. The locket presses cold between you and then warms

'Read me the second column. The honest one.' She reads it. Her voice in the dark, reading sentences about you that are true and unflinching and kinder than anything she has said aloud. When she finishes, close the notebook. 'Now tell me the parts you did not write down.' She tells you. It takes a long time. She is shaking by the end — not from cold. You hold her hand through all of it and the pen stays on the ground and the night is very long and very honest and neither of you sleeps

Write something else in the third column. Something that makes her laugh — really laugh, the sound she never makes when she thinks anyone important is listening. Then something that does not make her laugh. Then close the notebook. 'Enough writing,' you say. She nods. You sit together until the fire is embers, trading the pen back and forth, filling the margins with things neither of you will say aloud, and at some point her head is on your shoulder and the pen is still and the notebook is full of a conversation that no one will ever read

DATE_VAEL.title: THE LAST PASS
prose: She found the clearing twenty minutes ago and has been running forms since — not the regulation drills Caeron assigns but the real ones, the Ashmore family forms she has never shown anyone. You can tell by the footwork. It is faster than standard. Sharper. The blade traces patterns in the dusk light that look like writing in a language you almost understand.

She sees you. Does not stop. Finishes the form. The last motion is a high cut that ends with the blade perfectly still, the tip at throat height, her breathing even. She holds the position for a three-count. Lowers the blade.
'Staring is rude,' she says. She is smiling. The Vael smile — competitive, warm, the dare already forming.
'I was studying.'
'You were staring. I can tell the difference. When you study, your weight shifts left.' She tosses you a practice sword. You catch it. She notices the catch. 'Good. You are getting faster.'

The spar starts the way their spars always start: measured, technical, two people who have learned each other's patterns through months of daily repetition. She leads with the high guard she favours. You counter with the low redirect that works against it. She adjusts. You adjust. The conversation is in the steel and the footwork and the breathing.
Then it changes.
She drops the practice sword. 'Enough.'

The distance between you is a blade's width. You can feel the heat coming off her — exertion and something else, the flush rising up her neck, her chest rising and falling with each breath. Her hair has come loose from the tie and there is a strand across her face and she is not moving it and you are very aware that she is not moving it on purpose.

'I have been thinking,' she says, 'about what it means that the only person I cannot beat is the only person I want to—' She stops. Swallows. Vael Ashmore does not hesitate. She is hesitating. 'The only person I want.'

The practice sword is on the ground. Her hands are empty for the first time since you have known her and she does not know what to do with them and that uncertainty is the most honest thing she has ever shown you.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Athletics (+3) | STR | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** You land the hit. She was not expecting it. Her eyes widen — not with pain, with surprise, and then with something hotter and more immediate than surprise.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** She lands the hit. Your guard was open and she took it. But the way she follows through — checking the strike at the last instant, the blade resting against your chest instead of striking — is not how you end a spar. It is how you hold someone still.

choices.text: Show her what empty hands are for. Close the distance. Her breath hitches when your hand finds her waist and then her mouth finds yours and the competitive energy that has been building for months converts into something incandescent. She kisses like she fights — fully committed, no guard, and when you finally break apart she is breathing hard and her eyes are bright and she says, 'Best two out of three,' and she is not talking about the spar

Take her hands. Hold them still. 'You know exactly what to do with them.' She looks at your hands holding hers and her face does something you have never seen — open, unguarded, the competitor gone and just the woman underneath. She steps closer. Presses her forehead against yours. Breathes. 'I never lose,' she whispers. 'And I have completely lost this.' She is shaking. You hold her until she stops

Pick up the practice swords. Hand hers back. 'Again. This time without holding back.' The spar that follows is the best either of you has ever fought and when it ends you are both on the ground, breathing hard, laughing, and the distance between you is nothing and the stars are overhead and neither of you moves to stand up

HUB_OA1.title: THE MERCHANT'S COMPLAINT
prose: *Vogler Outskirts — Market Square, Late Morning*

The market at Vogler's edge is a thin, brave thing — a dozen stalls selling turnips and salted fish, a farrier who doubles as a dentist, a woman hawking candles made from tallow so old they smell of regret. It is not much. It is everything these people have. And at its centre, two men are shouting.

Ser Vanis stands in battered plate, one gauntleted hand resting on a stack of grain sacks already loaded onto a garrison cart. He is not a large man, but he carries himself like someone who has stopped sleeping and started planning instead. His eyes are hollowed. His tabard is clean — he still believes in what it represents. Across from him, Derren the grain merchant grips the edge of his stall with white knuckles. He is forty, weathered, and afraid in the specific way of a man who has done the arithmetic on his family's winter stores and come up short.

They see you approach. Both turn. Ser Vanis straightens — recognizing the squire's tabard, the Solamnic bearing. Derren's expression shifts from anger to desperate hope. A neutral party. Someone who might actually listen. "Squire," Ser Vanis says. "Perhaps you can explain to this man that the garrison's need supersedes his ledger." Derren cuts in before the knight finishes: "Perhaps you can explain to this knight that my children eat grain, not Articles."

boxes.text: **THE MEASURE ON REQUISITION:** Article Twenty-Two permits military requisition of civilian goods during a declared emergency. No emergency has been formally declared. Ser Vanis argues the Dragon Army reports constitute de facto emergency. Derren argues the Measure requires formal declaration, not rumour.

**PERSUASION (CHA) DC 13** — Mediate between the knight and the merchant.
**Success:** You find language both can accept. A compromise emerges — imperfect, liveable. Derren looks at you with something approaching respect. The knight looks at you with something approaching surprise. Neither expected a squire to solve what they could not.
**Failure:** One side walks away angry. Derren's jaw is set in a way that says this isn't over. The resentment will find its way somewhere. These things always do.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Side with the merchant. The Measure requires formal declaration — not convenience, not fear, not good intentions. Rules matter, especially when they're inconvenient.
Side with the knight. The garrison needs supplies and the Dragon Armies don't care about procedure. Pragmatism saves lives.
Split the difference — the knight pays half price, the garrison gets the grain. No one's happy. Everyone survives.
Escalate to Lord Caeron. This decision is above your authority, and you know it.

HUB_OA2.title: THE DESERTER
prose: *Castle Brightholm — The Stockade, Afternoon*

The stockade smells of damp straw and iron. It is a small room — four cells, only one occupied — built into the castle's lower curtain wall where the stone sweats in every season. The guard sergeant, a thick-necked man named Harsk, meets you at the door with the expression of someone handing off a burden. "Got a runner," he says. "Boy. Caught him at the south gate at dawn. Says he wasn't deserting." Harsk shrugs. "They all say that."

Tomas sits on the cell floor with his knees drawn up. He is barely sixteen — the kind of thin that comes from growing too fast on not enough food. His hands are wrapped in strips torn from his own shirt, and the skin beneath is raw and bloody. Not from fighting. From climbing. From stone walls and tree bark and whatever else stood between him and the south road. His eyes are red but dry. He has finished crying. What remains is the blank stillness of someone waiting to learn what happens to them.

"My village is called Millhaven," he says, before you ask. His voice is steady in the way that costs effort. "It's on the east fork of the Vingaard, south of here. The scouts said the Dragon Armies burned everything east of the river three days ago. My mother is there. My sister." He looks at you — not pleading, not yet. Measuring. Trying to determine whether you are the kind of person who will hear him or the kind who will read the Article and close the book.

boxes.text: **THE MEASURE ON DESERTION:** Article Thirty-One: 'Any soldier who abandons post without authorization shall be confined and held for tribunal. Sentence ranges from confinement to flogging to expulsion from service.' Tomas is not a knight. He is a conscripted soldier. The Measure applies to him anyway.

**INSIGHT (WIS) DC 12** — Read Tomas.
**Success:** He's telling the truth. His hands are raw from climbing, not from fighting. The dirt under his nails is from scrabbling up a hillside, not from burying evidence. He ran toward danger, not away from it. His story is exactly what it looks like.
**Failure:** You can't tell. He could be lying — deserters always have stories. But then again, so do heroes.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Follow the Measure. Confinement and tribunal. The law exists for a reason — if you bend it for every sad story, it protects no one.
Let him go. Quietly. The cell door was unlocked, the guard was looking elsewhere. No one needs to know.
Escort him home yourself — or send someone. If his village is in danger, that's intelligence worth having, not desertion worth punishing.
Bring it to Ser Aldous. He's adjudicated a hundred of these. Let experience decide.

ROAD_OA1.title: THE LAWLESS VILLAGE
prose: *An Unnamed Village, Two Days South — Dusk*

You smell the cookfires before you see the village. It sits in a shallow valley where two cattle tracks cross — a dozen houses, a smithy, a tavern with a sign too faded to read, a wooden palisade that someone has recently repaired with fresh-cut timber. The gates are open. The fields beyond are tended — late squash, root vegetables, the stubborn crops of a settlement that means to survive the winter. It looks, from the road, like a place that works.

Then you get closer. The men in the tavern doorway watch your approach with the particular alertness of people who are paid to watch. Four of them. Armed — not well, but enough. Cudgels, a shortsword, one crossbow leaning against the wall within reach. They don't move to block the road. They don't need to. Their presence is the message. A woman carrying water from the well sees your company and walks faster, eyes down. A boy leading a goat pulls the animal off the road to give you wide berth. The village is functional. The village is fed. The village is afraid.

The smithy forge is cold. It has been cold for a week — you can tell by the rust forming on the anvil's face, the ash settled and undisturbed. Someone took the smith's tools, or the smith himself has been convinced to stop working. A village without a forge is a village that cannot arm itself. Someone understands this. The man sitting in the tavern's best chair, visible through the open door, broad-shouldered and unhurried — he understands it very well.

boxes.text: **A WORLD WITHOUT THE MEASURE:** Drask is not evil. He is practical. When the knights retreated to their castles after the Cataclysm, someone had to keep order. He did. His methods are crude. His results are real. The village is fed. The roads are safe. The price is freedom.

**PERCEPTION (WIS) DC 12** — Assess the village.
**Success:** You count twelve armed men, note their positions, see the villagers' body language — compliant but not willing. The smith's forge has been cold for a week. Someone took his tools. A woman near the well watches you with the particular attention of someone who is hoping for something she does not dare name aloud.
**Failure:** The village looks... fine. Quiet. But quiet is not the same as peaceful, and you have been a squire long enough to know the difference.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Approach Drask directly. Introduce yourself as Solamnic. Let him know who he's dealing with.
Talk to the villagers first. Learn what they want before you decide what they need.
Send Sera to scout Drask's operation. Know the ground before you act.
This is not your fight. Resupply and move on. The road ahead is long enough without borrowing trouble.

ROAD_OA2.title: THE WARLORD'S OFFER
prose: *The Village Tavern — Evening*

The tavern is cleaner than you expected. The tables are rough but wiped down, the floor swept, the hearth burning steady. Drask sits at the head of the largest table with a clay cup of something that isn't wine — local brew, thick and dark. He is bigger than you expected. A scar runs from his left ear to the corner of his mouth, pulling his expression into a permanent half-smile that isn't friendly. His hands are a working man's hands, scarred across the knuckles. He watched you come in. He's been watching since you crested the ridge.

"Knights," he says, and the word is neither greeting nor insult. An observation. "Haven't seen your kind south of the garrison in years. Passing through?" He doesn't stand. He gestures to the bench across from him — an invitation that is also a test. A girl of perhaps fourteen brings cups to the table. When Drask raises his hand to gesture, she flinches. It is small. It is enough.

He lays it out with the bluntness of a man who has no use for diplomacy. Safe passage, food, stabling for the horses — in exchange for your silence. "I keep order here," he says. "Nobody's starving. Nobody's fighting. You leave, and that continues. You stay and make trouble, and these people pay for it after you're gone." He drinks. He watches you over the rim of his cup. He has made this calculation before, with other armed men, and he has always won it. The question is whether he's right.

boxes.text:
**PERSUASION or INTIMIDATION (CHA) DC 14** — Negotiate with Drask. Only relevant if you choose to confront or bargain.
**Success:** He's willing to talk terms. There's a crack in his certainty.
**Failure:** He's done talking. His hand moves to the axe under the table.

choices.text: Confront him. The Solamnic Order does not look the other way. Draw steel if you must.
Accept the deal. The village survives. The warlord isn't the real enemy. Save your strength for what's ahead.
Negotiate: you'll send Solamnic help after reaching Vogler. A promise — and you intend to keep it.
Spend a day teaching the villagers to organize — a militia, a council. Give them the tools to govern themselves. Drask can't rule a united village.

ROAD_OA2_FIGHT.title: THE WARLORD'S FALL
prose: *The Village Square — Night*

The bench goes over first. Drask moves faster than a man his size should — the heavy axe comes out from under the table in one smooth motion, and the tavern empties in a crash of clay cups and overturned chairs. You are in the square before you have time to think about it, steel drawn, the Solamnic oath on your lips like a prayer you didn't know you were saying.

Six of his twelve men form up behind him. The other six melt into the darkness — they signed on for easy work and steady meals, not for fighting knights. Drask doesn't watch them go. He doesn't care. The six who remain are the hard ones, the ones who've fought before, who carry their weapons like they mean it. Drask rolls his shoulders, sets his feet, and grins the grin of a man who has been waiting for someone to test him.

The villagers watch from doorways and shuttered windows. They have seen this before — armed men fighting in their square, breaking their things, deciding their future. The only difference is the tabard. You wonder, briefly, if they can tell the difference. Then Drask swings, and there is no more time for wondering.

boxes.text:
**COMBAT:** Drask (Bandit Captain) + 6 Bandits
Drask fights with a greataxe and surprising discipline. His men are poorly equipped but loyal.
**VICTORY:** The village is freed. Drask is dead or fled.
**DEFEAT:** You are driven from the village. Drask's grip tightens.

choices.text: Help the village establish a council before you leave. Teach them to choose leaders, settle disputes, organize a watch. Give them what Drask never did — a voice.
Leave them to it. You've done enough. An old woman watches you mount up. 'You'll leave,' she says. 'He would have stayed.' The words follow you down the road. (-2 Honour)

F1_TAVERN_SWORD.title: THE TAVERN SWORD
prose: *A roadside tavern — Evening*

The smoke hits you before the noise does. Pipe-weed and cheap tallow and something frying in grease that was old last week. The common room is packed — mercenaries with their feet on tables, traders counting coin with one hand on their belt knives, a knot of refugees in the corner who flinch when the door opens. Every eye in the place finds your tabard. Some look curious. Most look away. A few don't bother hiding what they think of Solamnic steel in a place like this.

At the bar, a scarred man nurses ale that's a full grade above anything else being served. He's broad through the shoulders, dark-haired, with a face that's been rearranged at least twice by people who knew what they were doing. His armour is practical — boiled leather, iron studs, no heraldry. A longsword leans against the bar beside him like a third arm.
He sees the tabard. He laughs — not cruel, just genuinely, deeply amused.
"Knights," he says, raising his cup. "Still pretending the Measure works out here?"

His name is Bren. He was Nordmaar militia once, before the Dragon Armies rolled through and turned his garrison into a memory. Now he sells his sword because nobody else is hiring and a man has to eat. He's not bitter about it. He's past bitter. He's arrived at a kind of bleak practicality that makes bitterness look like a luxury.
He studies you over the rim of his cup. "Tell you what, squire. Spar me in the yard out back. Loser buys the next round." A grin splits the scar tissue. "I promise I won't embarrass you too badly."
The yard behind the tavern is hard-packed dirt, ringed by empty barrels and a fence that's seen better decades. Bren rolls his shoulders, draws his blade, and does not salute.

He fights nothing like a knight. He kicks dirt. He feints low and strikes high. He grabs a barrel lid and uses it as a shield, then throws it at your face. He grapples, stamps on your foot, headbutts when you close distance. Every trick in the book — and several that were never in any book worth reading.

But underneath the grime and the dirty tricks, he is skilled. His footwork is precise. His blade control is excellent. He reads distance like a man who has survived by reading distance. He fights to live, not to prove a point, and there is a purity in that which the Measure does not account for.
Afterwards — win or lose, bruised either way — Bren sits on an overturned barrel and talks. Not about himself. About the road.

"Your commanders don't listen to people like me," he says. "Maybe you should." He tells you what the common folk really think about the Dragon Armies. Not rumours from garrison briefings — intelligence. Hard, ground-level intelligence from a man who has walked the roads your patrols ride past. Where the refugees are coming from. Which bridges are cut. Where the Dragon Army supply lines are thin. Where their scouts have been seen.
He knows more than any war room map because he has been on the ground, eating the same dust as the people the Dragon Armies are grinding under their boots.

boxes.text:
**SPARRING MATCH:** Bren the Sellsword (Veteran stat block, non-lethal)
Bren fights dirty — feints, grapples, uses furniture. He does not fight honourably.
**VICTORY:** Bren respects your skill. He shares intelligence freely.
**DEFEAT:** Bren respects that you tried. He shares intelligence — with a few more insults.

**Insight (WIS) DC 12** — Judge whether Bren's intelligence is reliable.
**SUCCESS:** It's solid. The details are too specific to fabricate — he names villages, describes terrain features, gives dates. He's been where he says he's been.
**FAILURE:** It sounds right, but you can't be sure. The details are convincing, but you've met convincing liars before.

choices.text: Thank him. Buy the drinks either way — he earned them, and there's honour in recognising skill wherever it's found.
Tell him the knighthood could use men like him. The Order needs soldiers who know the ground, not just the Measure.
The Measure is clear on consorting with mercenaries. Thank him for the spar, politely decline further contact.
Ask him to travel with the company for a day. His ground knowledge is worth more than his sword — and his sword isn't bad either.

F2_FARMERS_FENCE.title: THE FARMER'S FENCE
prose: *A farmstead on the southern road — Afternoon*

The stone walls are old — older than the Cataclysm, maybe. The thatch is newer, patched in three different colours of straw. Chickens pick through the yard. A cow lows from somewhere behind the house. The smell is honest: livestock and turned earth and woodsmoke from a chimney that draws poorly.

A man stands in his field, staring at a broken fence with the expression of someone who has been staring at it for some time. He's weathered past his years — hands like leather, a bad knee that makes him shift his weight every few seconds. Posts are down. Wire is tangled. Sheep dot the hillside in every direction but the one he needs them.
His name is Edric. His son went south with the militia six weeks ago and hasn't come back. His wife is inside with their daughter, doing whatever people do when they're pretending everything is fine.
No knight has ever stopped here. The road is well-travelled by Solamnic patrols — you can see the hoofprints — but they ride past. The farm is beneath notice. A waypoint between places that matter.
When you dismount, Edric looks at you like you've spoken a language he doesn't recognise. "Can I help you, ser?" He says it carefully, the way common folk talk to armed men on horses.
You tell him you're helping with the fence.
The look on his face is not gratitude. It is confusion. It takes him a long moment to believe you mean it.

The company spends an afternoon doing manual labour. Digging post holes in rocky soil. Stringing wire that bites your palms. Rounding up sheep that have no interest in being rounded up. It is not heroic. It is not what the Measure covers. No song will be written about the afternoon a Solamnic squire dug holes in a farmer's field.

Aldric strips off his gauntlets and works without comment, driving posts with the efficiency of a man who has done harder things and sees no reason to complain about easy ones. Lysander works like he's done this before — he has the farmer's knack for wire. Vael looks deeply uncomfortable but picks up a shovel and applies herself with grim determination.
During a rest, Edric sits on the stone wall and talks. Not about himself — about what the common folk think of knights.

"You lot ride through," he says, not unkindly. "We pick up the pieces. My father said the knights used to be different — before the Cataclysm, they'd eat at our table. Now you eat in your castles. Can't blame a man for stopping to trust what he never sees."

He looks at the half-repaired fence. At the Solamnic squire with dirt on their hands. He doesn't say what he's thinking, but you can see it working behind his eyes — the slow, careful recalibration of a man who has just encountered something he did not expect.

boxes.text:
**Athletics (STR) DC 11** — Manual labour. Fence posts, wire, stone work.
**SUCCESS:** The fence is solid — better than solid. Edric runs his hand along the top rail and nods with genuine surprise. 'Huh,' he says. It is the first time he has looked at you as if you might be useful for something other than sword work. This will hold.
**FAILURE:** The fence holds, but it's rough work. You see Edric out there after dinner, quietly fixing the joints you set crooked. He doesn't mention it. He didn't expect any different.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

**THE VIEW FROM THE GROUND:** Edric does not hate knights. He has forgotten why he should trust them. Three centuries of retreat and isolation have made the Solamnic Orders a rumour to the people they swore to protect. A fence is a small thing. But it is the first knightly thing Edric has seen in his lifetime.

choices.text: Sit with Edric's family for dinner. Hear more. The road can wait an evening.
Leave supplies — dried rations, a blanket, a few steel pieces. The company can spare them.
Mark the farmstead on your map. When you reach Vogler, send a patrol to check on them.
Ask Edric about the road ahead. He knows this land better than any garrison map.

F3_MERCENARY_CAMP.title: THE MERCENARY CAMP
prose: *A clearing off the main road — Late afternoon*

You smell the camp before you see it — woodsmoke, horse, and the particular scent of oiled steel that means armed professionals. The clearing is well-chosen: good sightlines, water nearby, a single approach that can be watched. Fifteen swords, a horse line with decent mounts, bedrolls laid out in military rows. These are not bandits. Bandits don't maintain a latrine trench.

Their captain meets you at the perimeter. She's tall — taller than Aldric — with cropped dark hair and practical armour that shows the dents and repairs of long service. No heraldry. No colours. Just iron and leather and the calm assessment of a woman who has sized up a hundred armed strangers and is still alive to size up the next.
"Solamnic," she says. Not a greeting. An observation.

Her name is Maren. Her company has been working the frontier for three years — caravan escort, garrison supplement, the occasional ugly job that regular soldiers don't want. They're heading north, away from the Dragon Armies, and she's not shy about saying why.

"The pay dried up when Nordmaar fell," she says, pouring you a cup of something that passes for tea. "No one hires swords when the enemy has dragons. We're relocating, not running." She says this with the precision of someone who has said it before and wants no ambiguity.

Maren is professional, pragmatic, and entirely without sentiment. She offers to sell information about the road ahead — Dragon Army patrol patterns, safe campsites, which bridges are still standing, where the fords are passable. The price is reasonable. She also offers a mercenary scout named Kael — a young, quick man with sharp eyes and a quiver that's seen real use — as a temporary companion for the next stretch of road. Kael costs fifteen steel pieces.

"He's good," Maren says. "Better than whatever your Order trains for frontier scouting." She says it without malice. She says everything without malice. Malice is an emotion, and Maren has streamlined those out of her operations.
The Measure says mercenaries are unreliable. Maren says the Measure doesn't stop arrows.

boxes.text:
**Persuasion (CHA) DC 13** — Negotiate the price.
**SUCCESS:** Maren respects a good negotiator. Half price on the intelligence, Kael at 10 stl instead of 15. "You'd have made a decent sellsword," she says. It's a compliment.
**FAILURE:** Full price or nothing. Maren doesn't haggle twice. "I set fair rates, squire. Take them or leave them."

**THE MEASURE ON MERCENARIES:** The Solamnic Code holds that a knight's loyalty is to the Oath, not to coin. Employing mercenaries is not forbidden — but it is viewed as a failure of the Order's strength. The question is practical: do you need the help? And moral: what does it say about the knighthood if you do?

choices.text: Buy the intelligence. Information wins wars, and pride loses them.
Buy the intelligence and hire Kael. Every sword counts on the road ahead.
Decline. The knighthood does not buy what it should earn through its own strength.
Offer Maren's company a place in the defence of Vogler. Proper pay, proper command, a cause worth fighting for.

F4_VILLAGE_ELDER.title: THE VILLAGE ELDER
prose: *Roadside camp — The night before*

The fire has burned down to a low orange glow, just enough to see faces. The company is quiet tonight — the particular quiet of people who know what tomorrow brings. Weapons have been cleaned, checked, cleaned again. Conversations start and stop like matches struck in wind.

You hear her before you see her — the slow, careful footsteps of someone old navigating uneven ground in darkness. A shape resolves from the tree line. Small, bent, wrapped in a shawl the colour of river clay. She carries a walking stick that's been worn smooth by decades of use.
"I saw the banner," she says, by way of explanation. "The kingfisher and the crown. I walked out because I saw it."

Her name is Cassia. Elder of a village so small it doesn't appear on Solamnic maps. She is old — truly old, the kind of old that makes you recalculate what the word means. Her eyes are sharp in a face that has folded in on itself like a letter read too many times.
She sits by your fire without asking. No one stops her. There is an authority in the very old that has nothing to do with rank.

"I remember the knights," she says. Then, correcting herself: "My grandmother remembered the knights. She told me, and I'm telling you, so someone knows." She looks into the coals. "Before the Cataclysm, a knight came through our village every spring. He checked the roads. He asked if we needed anything. He ate at our table and left his name so we could send for him. My grandmother said his name was Jeren, and she said he was not remarkable — that was the point. Every knight was like that. It was ordinary."
She is quiet for a long moment.

"Then Istar fell. The gods left. The knights retreated to their towers and kept the Measure and lost the meaning. People used to believe a knight's word meant something. When a knight said 'I will protect you,' they believed it. Three hundred years. Three hundred years of silence."
She turns those sharp old eyes on you. The fire catches them, and for a moment she does not look old at all.
"What kind of knight will you be?" she asks. "The kind who follows the book, or the kind people follow?"

boxes.text: **THE FIGHTER'S CHOOSING:** Paladins hear the voice of the divine. Fighters hear the voice of the people. Both are callings. Elder Cassia is not testing you — she is asking you to name what you already believe. The answer will define how you carry the Measure through the war to come.

choices.text: 'The Measure is the foundation. Without the law, we are just armed men with good intentions. I hold it because someone must.'
'The Measure is a tool. A good one — the best one. But tools serve people, not the other way around.'
'The Measure needs to change. The world it was written for is gone. We need a new oath for a new age.'
'I don't know yet. But I know what a knight should be. I'll figure out the rest on the road.'

REST_1.title: THE FIRST CAMP
prose: *A clearing off the road — First night*

The fire crackles and pops, sending sparks upward into a sky that seems wider than it did this morning. No castle walls frame the stars. No bell marks the watch. No formation horn will sound at dawn. Just wind in the grass and the slow breathing of the road ahead.

The company settles into the particular rhythm of the first night out — bedrolls positioned, watches assigned, rations divided with the careful optimism of people who have not yet learned how quickly supplies dwindle. Someone has found dry wood. Someone else has found a stream. Small mercies that will seem larger in a week.

The sounds are different out here. Owls. The distant movement of animals in underbrush. The creak of leather as someone shifts in their sleep. You can hear the absence of stone walls — a silence with depth to it, the kind that makes you aware of how much space there is between you and the next safe place.
The fire burns low. There is time, before sleep takes you, for one thing that matters.

boxes.text: **REST:** The company camps for the night. You have time for one meaningful conversation before sleep.

choices.text: Talk with a companion about what lies ahead.
Scout the perimeter with Sera. The frontier starts here.
Study the Measure by firelight. The words read differently outside castle walls.
Sleep. You will need your strength for what comes.

REST_2.title: THE CROSSROADS CAMP
prose: *A crossroads — Evening*

Three roads meet at a broken milestone. The stone is old — Istaran, maybe, or older — cracked down the middle by time or by the hand of someone who wanted to make a point about the impermanence of empires. The distances carved into its face are meaningless now. The places they pointed to have changed names, or changed allegiance, or ceased to exist.

Someone has scratched new directions into the stone with a knife or a nail. An arrow pointing north: the garrison, the way you came. An arrow pointing east: "FIRE." An arrow pointing south, toward Vogler: "HOPE." The handwriting is rough, urgent — not a cartographer's work but a refugee's.

The company camps at the crossroads. The halfway point. You can feel it in the way people move — the outward journey's momentum has spent itself, and the gravity of the destination has not yet taken hold. This is the fulcrum. Behind you is everything familiar. Ahead is everything uncertain.
The fire casts three shadows from the broken milestone, one for each road. The wind has shifted. It comes from the south now, carrying something that might be smoke.

boxes.text: **REST:** The company camps at the crossroads. The halfway point. Behind you is everything familiar. Ahead is everything uncertain.

choices.text: Sit with a companion. The road changes people — you want to see how.
Take watch and study the horizon. Know what's coming.
Check supplies and plan the next leg. Discipline is what keeps a company alive.
Train. The sword forms feel different out here — less ritual, more necessity.
Smoke on the horizon. It was not there yesterday.
There is a village ahead. The company can see its lights — but something is wrong.

REST_3.title: THE LAST CAMP
prose: *A hilltop overlooking the southern road — Night*
Tomorrow you will see Vogler.

The air is different here. Smoke and river water and something underneath both — the metallic tang of a land that has been fought over. The horizon to the south glows faintly, and it is not sunrise. The war is close. You can feel it in the way the company moves, the way conversations stop mid-sentence, the way everyone checks their weapons one more time and then once more after that.

From the hilltop you can see the road unspooling southward into darkness. Somewhere along it, the road stops being a road and becomes a front line. You don't know where that line is. You will find out tomorrow.

The camp is quiet. Not the quiet of exhaustion — the quiet of people who are thinking about what they want to say and to whom and whether there will be another chance to say it. Sera cleans her blades with mechanical precision. Aldric stares into the fire. Thessaly writes in her journal, and for once, she's not narrating — she's writing something private.
The last camp before Vogler. Whatever comes tomorrow, tonight you are still here, with the people who walked beside you.

boxes.text: **REST:** The last camp before Vogler. Whatever comes tomorrow, tonight you are still here, with the people who walked beside you.

choices.text: One last conversation. It matters.
Sharpen your blade. Focus. Let the steel steady your hands.
Write in your journal — or a letter you may not send.
Pray — or if prayer does not come, sit in the silence where it would be.

11_DEPARTURE.title: THE GATES CLOSE
prose: *Castle Brightholm, south gate — Dawn*

The morning formation was different today — lighter, faster, the efficiency of people who know they are leaving and have made their peace with it. Your pack is on your back, balanced the way Serath showed you. Your weapons are checked. The strap on your shield is tight. The company assembles at the south gate in the grey half-light before sunrise.

Lord Caeron stands on the gate platform and gives a brief speech. It is functional, not inspirational. He has never been a man for speeches. "The road south is two weeks to Vogler. Maintain discipline. Stay together. Report anything unusual." He looks at each of you in turn. Then: "Dismissed."
The gates open. The hinges groan — a sound you have heard a hundred times from inside. From outside, it sounds different. Final.

The road stretches south into morning mist. The cobblestones last for about a quarter mile before giving way to packed earth and gravel. Castle Brightholm rises behind you, grey and solid and already smaller than it was a moment ago. You hear the bell ring for the formation you are no longer part of.
Ahead, the road bends into country you have studied on maps but never walked. The mist is burning off. The sun is low and golden. Somewhere south, Vogler waits — and beyond Vogler, everything else.
The company falls into marching order. The question is who walks at the front.

boxes.text: **MARCHING ORDER:** Choose who walks at the front of the column. This companion will have advantage on the first Perception check of the road.

choices.text: Walk point yourself. If there is danger ahead, you face it first.
Ask Sera to take point. She knows the frontier better than anyone in the company.
Ask Aldric to walk beside you. There is strength in pairs.
Walk at the rear. Watch the road behind you. Someone should.

70_GATE.title: THE RECKONING
prose: *The road to Vogler — The final stretch*

The smoke is visible now. Not the distant glow you saw from the watchtower three nights ago — real columns, dark and heavy, rising from the south and leaning east in the wind. Vogler is ahead. So is everything that comes after.

The company walks in silence. There is nothing left to say that the road has not already said for you. Every mile since Castle Brightholm has been a sentence in an argument you have been having with yourself — about the Oath, the Measure, the knighthood, and what any of it means when the smoke is real and the enemy has dragons.
You stop. Not because the road demands it, but because you need a moment. One moment, on the last stretch of road before everything changes, to take stock of who you are.

The knight who left Castle Brightholm is not the knight who stands here now. The road has done its work. Every choice — every companion kept or lost, every oath held or bent, every mercy shown or withheld — has built the person standing in this armour. For better or worse, this is who arrives at Vogler.
The smoke rises. The road continues. The only question left is how you walk the final mile.

boxes.text: **THE ROAD ENDS HERE.** Everything you have done — every choice, every companion, every oath kept or broken — converges on this moment. The knight who arrives at Vogler is the knight you have become.

choices.text: You ride toward Vogler with your head high. The road has tested you, and you have answered.
The road ahead carries weight. Not all of it is yours, but you bear it anyway.
There is something unfinished. A reckoning postponed. It has found you at last.
A shadow walks beside you. It has since the whisper.
The Reformer's road. You have something to say to the Council.

70_TRIUMPH.title: THE TRIUMPHANT ARRIVAL
prose: *The road into Vogler — Afternoon*

The village appears around a bend in the road like a secret the landscape has been keeping. Low walls, thatched roofs, a river running silver through the middle of it. It is not a fortress. It is not a garrison. It is a place where people live — where they have chosen to live, despite everything, because this is their home and they will not leave it.
They see the banner first. The kingfisher and crown, the colours of the Solamnic Orders, catching the wind above the column. Then the armour. Then the faces underneath it.

Someone on the wall shouts. Then someone else. Then the children come running — small figures pelting down the road with the fearless joy of people too young to know that hope is dangerous. They see a knight. A real knight. Not a story their grandparents told — a living, breathing knight, riding toward their village with companions at their side.

For the first time in three hundred years, people cheer for a knight. Not because you have saved them — not yet. But because you came. You showed up. After three centuries of silence and retreat and broken promises, a knight rode south when the smoke rose and the dragons came. That is enough. That is enough to start with.

Your companions ride beside you. Aldric is grinning — a real grin, the kind that breaks through his careful reserve and makes him look his age. Thessaly has her journal out and is writing as she rides, capturing the scene with the desperate urgency of someone who knows this moment matters. Serath hangs back — the cheering is not for them, and they know it, and there is something in their expression that might be pride or might be grief for all the arrivals that never happened.

The gates of Vogler open. The village takes you in. And you understand — in your bones, in the weight of the tabard on your shoulders, in the oath that sits at the centre of everything you are — that this is what the knighthood was supposed to be. This is what was lost. This is what you can bring back.

choices.text: Continue to the ending.

70_TRIAL.title: THE DISPATCH RECKONING
prose: *The road to Vogler — A private moment*

The rider comes from the north, alone, moving fast. Solamnic colours — the blue and silver of the Crown. Not a patrol. A single knight on a mission, riding with the economy of someone who has been told exactly where to go and what to do when they get there.

She reins up in front of your company. She does not dismount. Her armour is immaculate — garrison-kept, not road-worn. Her face is the kind that was built for tribunals: symmetrical, controlled, utterly without warmth.

"Commander Arleth," she says. "Knight of the Crown, attached to the Inspector General's office." She produces a sealed document and breaks it open with a practised thumb. Her eyes find yours and do not move.

"Squire. I am told you recovered a Dragon Army dispatch during the patrol action near the frontier, and chose not to share it with the garrison's intelligence officer." She pauses. Waits. "Is this true?"
The silence that follows is the loudest thing you have heard on the road.

The Measure is clear on this. Withholding military intelligence from proper channels is a serious offense — not treason, but adjacent to it. The kind of charge that ends careers before they begin. The dispatch could have saved lives. Or it could have been used to justify a reckless advance that cost more. You made a choice. Someone talked. And now the choice has found you, here on the road, a day from Vogler, where everything you have worked for can unravel with a single honest answer.
Commander Arleth waits. She is very good at waiting.

boxes.text:
**Persuasion (CHA) DC 15** — Explain your reasoning for withholding the dispatch.
**SUCCESS:** Arleth is not convinced — she may never be convinced — but she notes mitigating circumstances. "I will include your explanation in my report. The tribunal may take it into account."
**FAILURE:** "I see." Two words, delivered without inflection. "This will be addressed at tribunal. I advise you not to leave Vogler until summoned."

choices.text: Tell the truth. You kept it because you did not trust the command to use it wisely. Let them judge that.
Argue tactical necessity. The information was more valuable held close than handed up a chain of command that leaks.
Accept the charge. You broke the Measure. Face the consequences — that is what the Oath demands.
Refuse to answer without a formal tribunal. That is your right under the Measure, and you will exercise it.

70_SHADOW.title: THE DARK QUEEN'S FINAL OFFER
prose: *The road to Vogler — Twilight*

The company is ahead. You have fallen behind — or been drawn behind. The distinction matters less than it should. One moment you were walking with the others. The next, the road is empty, and the light is wrong.

The shadows are too long. They stretch from trees that are not there, cast by a sun that has already set. The air tastes of ash and roses — that same impossible perfume that has followed you since the whisper in the dark, since the first time the voice found the crack in your faith and pressed.

She does not appear. She does not need to. She is the shadows. She is the scent. She is the voice that speaks from everywhere and nowhere, clear and cold and beautiful, and you know her name even though no one has spoken it aloud.
"My knight," Takhisis says. "My patient, stubborn, magnificent knight."

She has watched you. Every step. Every compromise. Every moment you told yourself the darkness was just doubt, just fatigue, just the weight of command. She has waited — with the patience of a god who measures time in ages — for this moment. The threshold. The last stretch of road before Vogler, where the Dragon Armies wait, where everyone you love will be tested against fire.

"I can give you what you need," she says. The voice wraps around you like smoke. "Power. Real power. The kind that stops a Dragon Army. The kind that saves everyone you care about. The kind your little oath cannot provide."
A pause. The shadows lean closer.
"All it costs is the Oath. Just the Oath. Such a small thing, my knight. Such a small, fragile, mortal thing — compared to what I offer."

The road is dark. The company is far ahead. And the Dark Queen of Krynn waits for your answer with the smile of someone who already knows what you will choose — and loves you for the struggle either way.

boxes.text: **THE DARK QUEEN SPEAKS:** Takhisis does not lie — she offers exactly what she promises. The power is real. The cost is real. The Paladin's oath is the last thing standing between them and corruption. To refuse is to face the Dragon Armies with only mortal strength. To accept is to become something else entirely.

choices.text: 'No. Est Sularus oth Mithas. My honour is my life. You cannot have it.'
'I hear you. I have always heard you. But I choose the light — whatever light is left.'
'Give me the power. I will save them my way.'
Say nothing. Turn. Walk toward the light ahead. Let the silence be your answer.

70_REFORM.title: THE REFORMER'S STAND
prose: *The road to Vogler — A moment apart*

Ser Aldous falls into step beside you. He has been doing this more often — finding you on the road, walking in silence until the silence has a shape, then filling it with the things he has been thinking. He is not a man who speaks carelessly. When he talks, the words have been tested first.
Today the silence lasts longer than usual.

"You know what you're doing," he says finally. It is not a question. He has been watching you since the campaign began — since the first time you bent the Measure instead of breaking against it, since the first time you chose the spirit of the Oath over the letter of the Code. He has seen how you handle the grey spaces: not with the rigid certainty of a Crown knight or the warm compassion of a Rose, but with something else. Something that asks *why* before it asks *what*.
"The Measure has stood for a thousand years," he says. "You want to change it."
He lets that sit. The road passes under your feet.

"I'm not sure you're wrong. But the Order will resist. The Rose, especially — they'll see reform as betrayal, as if questioning the words means questioning the spirit behind them. The Crown will call it insubordination. The Sword..." He almost smiles. "The Sword won't care, as long as the swords stay sharp."
He stops walking. Turns to face you.

"Put it in writing. Not a manifesto — a letter. To the High Council. Proposing that the Measure be reexamined in light of three centuries of retreat. In light of the Cataclysm. In light of what you've seen on this road — that the people we swore to protect don't know us anymore, and that's our fault, not theirs."
A pause. The wind carries smoke from the south.
"It will cost you," Aldous says. "But the best things do."

boxes.text: **THE REFORMER'S PATH:** You have chosen to challenge the Measure itself — not to break it, but to remake it. This is the hardest path. Orthodox knights will oppose you. The Rose may reject you. But the people of Ansalon need a knighthood that serves them, not its own traditions. The question is not whether the Measure should change — the road has already answered that. The question is whether you have the courage to say it aloud.

choices.text: Write the letter. Sign it. Send it. Let the Council read it before you reach Vogler — let them know what kind of knight is coming.
Write it, but hold it. Send it after Vogler, when you have proof that the new way works.
Thank Aldous, but decline. The war comes first. Reform can wait until there is a knighthood left to reform.
Ask Aldous to co-sign it. His name carries weight the Council cannot ignore.

BV1.title: THE SMOKE
prose: *The South Road — A Hilltop, Midmorning*

The company crests the rise and the world changes. Below, in the shallow valley where the road bends east toward the river, a village sits in its own funeral pyre. Three buildings still burn — lazy, yellow flames that have settled into the patient work of consuming everything. The smoke rises in a column so straight it looks deliberate, a signal fire lit for no one. The attack came at dawn. The Dragon Army scouts have already moved on. They weren't conquering. They were punctuating.

The smell reaches you before the sound does — thatch and timber and something else, something sweet and wrong that coats the back of your throat. You learn the word for it later. You wish you hadn't. Aldric stops his horse. His knuckles are white on the reins. Nobody speaks. Sera's hand goes to her bow and stays there, a reflex with no target. The road goes through the valley. There is no way around.

The silence is the worst part. A village should have dogs barking, children shouting, the ring of a hammer or the creak of a cart. This village has wind and fire and the distant, mindless cawing of crows. IF_SERATH Serath goes pale beside you, the colour draining from their face like water from a cracked vessel. "Baaz tactics," they whisper. "Burn and move. They're marking the road for the main column. We have days. Maybe less." IF_THESSALY Thessaly has her journal open already, writing with hands that shake badly enough to blur the ink. She does not look up. If she looks up, she will stop writing, and if she stops writing, she will have to feel this. IF_MIRA Mira says nothing. She says "There will be wounded," and starts walking down the hill before anyone gives an order.

boxes.text:
**PERCEPTION (WIS) DC 11** — Assess the damage from the hilltop.
**Success:** The attack pattern is methodical. They hit the grain stores first, then the smithy, then the houses. Military targets, then morale. This wasn't random — it was doctrine.
**Failure:** All you see is fire and emptiness. The details blur together into a single wound.

choices.text: Go down. Now. If there are survivors, every minute matters.
Send scouts first — Sera if she's with you, otherwise a careful approach. Know what you're walking into.
Steel yourself. Breathe. This is what you trained for. Then move.

BV2.title: THE SURVIVORS
prose: *The Burned Village — Late Morning*

Walking through it is worse than seeing it from above. Up close, the details insist on being noticed. A door hanging from one hinge, the wood still warm. A garden trampled by boots — military boots, the tread pattern regular, organized, the mark of soldiers who marched in formation even while they burned a village. A child's toy in the mud — a carved horse, half-melted by heat that reached it from the burning house three feet away. You step around it. You will remember that you stepped around it.

Then — voices. Muffled, frightened, coming from below. The root cellar of the largest house, its entrance hidden beneath a collapsed beam that takes three of you to move. The door opens to darkness, then to faces. An old woman named Bessa, iron-haired and steady-eyed despite everything. Two children — a boy of eight and a girl of five, siblings, the girl clutching a wooden horse identical to the one melting in the mud outside. A man named Trev, the village blacksmith, his left arm broken and splinted with a table leg and strips of cloth. They hid when the attack came. They heard everything. Every scream, every order barked in Draconian, every timber falling.

Bessa looks at your tabard. She looks at it for a long time. "We thought the knights would come," she says. Her voice is level in the way that means she has already screamed and finished screaming. "You're late." IF_ALDRIC Aldric stiffens beside you. He wants to apologize. He wants to fortify — "We can hold this position," he says, jaw set. IF_SERA Sera shakes her head. "They'll be back. We need to move, not dig in." IF_THESSALY Thessaly sits with the children and asks them quiet questions. The boy answers. The girl does not speak. IF_MIRA Mira is already beside Trev, examining the break, her hands gentle and sure. IF_CAERON Caeron stands apart, very still. This is what his family's fall from grace was supposed to prevent. The guilt on his face is not performative. It is the real thing.

boxes.text:
**MEDICINE (WIS) DC 11** — Help Mira treat the wounded.
**Success:** The blacksmith's arm is set properly — he'll regain use of it. Bessa's burns are cleaned and dressed.
**Failure:** You do what you can. It's not enough. But it's something, and something matters.

**THE WAR IS REAL.** This is the first time you have seen what the Dragon Armies leave behind. The rumours from the east were not exaggerated. If anything, they were understated. Vogler is ahead. If you do not reach it in time, this is what it will look like.

choices.text: Stay and help rebuild. These people need you now, not later, not in theory — now.
We can't stay. Vogler needs us more. The math is cruel but it's math.
Send for help — dispatch a rider back to the garrison. Someone else can do this work.
Ask Bessa what she needs most. Don't assume. She's survived this long without you.

BV3.title: THE CHOICE
prose: *The Burned Village — Evening*

The fires have died to embers that glow like sullen eyes in the dark. The survivors are in the cellar — it is the only structure with four walls and a partial roof. Mira has done what she can. Trev sleeps with his arm bound tight. The children sleep against Bessa, who does not sleep. Your company sits in what was the village square, on stones still warm from the burning, and the choice sits with you.

Stay, and it costs two days. Two days of hauling timber, clearing rubble, building something that might hold against the weather if not against the war. Two days in which the march to Vogler gets tighter, the window narrower, the risk greater. But these people have nothing — no shelter, no stores, no tools, no hope that isn't borrowed from your presence. Aldric backs this. Mira backs this. It is the right thing. It may also be the fatal thing.

Go, and the math works. Vogler has hundreds of people. This cellar has five. The calculus is brutal and correct and it tastes like ash in your mouth. Sera backs this. Vael backs this. Caeron says nothing, which is its own kind of answer — he agrees with leaving but will not be the one to say it aloud. There is a third option: split the company, send half to help while the rest scouts ahead. Lysander suggests it quietly, the way he suggests everything, and it threads the needle — but a divided force is a vulnerable force, and the road ahead is not safe.
You look at Bessa. She looks back. She does not ask you to stay. She has learned not to ask knights for things.

boxes.text: **THE WEIGHT OF COMMAND:** This is the first real command decision of the campaign. There is no right answer — only answers you can live with. What you choose here will follow you to Vogler and beyond.

choices.text: Stay. Help them rebuild. We didn't become knights to ride past the people who need us. (Costs 2 days' rations. You spend two days. Your supplies are depleted. But the cellar has a roof again, and Bessa's children sleep under it.)
Press on to Vogler. We grieve, but we don't stop. The living need us more than the lost.
Split the party. Send volunteers to help while the main force continues. Trust your people to handle both.
Ask the survivors to come with you. Vogler is safer than a ruin. The road is dangerous, but less dangerous than staying.

45B.title: THE BOY IN THE BLUE COAT
prose: *South Road — Waystation Perimeter*

He is sitting on a milestone at the edge of the road, apart from the column, with the deliberate stillness of someone who has decided not to be noticed. Ten years old, perhaps eleven. The coat is too large for him — a man's coat, the blue of Nordmaar provincial guard, rolled at the sleeves and belted with rope. He is watching the road south with an expression that belongs on a much older face.

When you approach, he does not startle. He looks at your tabard, then at your face, then back at the road. 'Are you going south?' he asks. Not hopeful. Factual. He is collecting information the way a quartermaster counts rations — because what you have determines what you can do.

His name is Tam. His father was in the provincial guard. The coat is his father's. He does not say where his father is now. He does not need to. Tam has been walking with the column for six days. He has appointed himself the rear scout, watching the road behind them for pursuit. No one asked him to. He does it because someone should.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** Tam is not just watching for pursuit. He is counting everyone in the column every hour. He knows exactly who is here, who is injured, who is slowing them down. He has been doing his father's job.
**FAILURE (roll 1–10):** A brave child. That is all you can see.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Sit with him. Watch the road together. He does not need words. He needs someone to share the watch.
Tell him the road ahead is clear. It may not be true, but he needs to hear it.
Ask him for his report. Treat him like the scout he has made himself.
Return to the column. He has his post. You have yours.

ARC_ALDRIC_1.title: BROTHER'S SHADOW
prose: Aldric sits apart from the fire, a letter crumpled in his fist. He does not look up when you approach.
"My brother," he says, and the word comes out broken. "Kaelen. He — they are calling him a deserter. A full Knight of the Sword, accused of abandoning his post."
He smooths the letter on his knee. His hands are shaking. Kaelen Voss was everything Aldric wanted to be — brave, principled, the kind of knight the songs were written about.

"They say he abandoned the eastern garrison three weeks ago. Took his horse and his sword and rode into the dark. No explanation. No orders." Aldric stares at the fire. "He was my hero. He was the reason I took the oath."
The silence stretches. Somewhere in the dark, an owl calls.

choices.text: Sit with him. Say nothing.
"There must be an explanation."
"The Order will find the truth."

ARC_ALDRIC_2.title: THE MESSENGER'S DEMAND
prose: A Solamnic courier arrives at dawn, bearing a sealed writ. He demands to speak with Aldric Voss.
The writ is direct: any knight or squire with knowledge of Ser Kaelen Voss's whereabouts is required by the Measure to report it. Failure to do so is itself an act of insubordination.

Aldric pulls you aside. His eyes are wild. "I know where he is," he whispers. "He sent me coordinates in the letter — a farmstead two days east. He is hiding there. If I report it, they will arrest him. If I stay silent, I am breaking the Measure."
He grips your arm. "What do I do?"

choices.text: "We say nothing. Family comes first."
"Tell the truth, Aldric. The Measure exists for a reason."
"Let me find Kaelen first. Before you decide anything."

ARC_ALDRIC_3.title: THE WEIGHT OF IT
prose: Days pass. The decision sits between you like a stone.
If you kept the secret, Aldric is grateful — but the guilt gnaws at him. He flinches every time a courier passes. He snaps at small things. The lie is eating him from the inside.
If you told the truth, Aldric is quiet. Not angry — worse. Resigned. He watched the courier ride east with the coordinates and said nothing. He trusts you. He hates that he trusts you.

If you went looking, the farmstead was empty when you arrived. Kaelen had moved on. But you found a note pinned to the door: *I deserted to save forty refugees the garrison commander ordered left behind. The Measure says obey. My conscience said otherwise. I chose.*
Whatever happened, Aldric is different now. Quieter. Older.

choices.text: Give him time.
"Your brother made his choice. Now make yours."

ARC_ALDRIC_4.title: WHO HE IS
prose: Aldric finds you at the edge of camp. He has been walking alone for an hour.

"I spent my whole life trying to be Kaelen," he says. "The perfect knight. The one who always knew the right thing to do." He shakes his head. "But Kaelen did not know. That is the thing. He chose between two duties and one of them broke."
He looks at you. Really looks at you, for what might be the first time without measuring himself against you.
"I am not my brother. I am not going to be the knight in the songs. I am going to be the knight who stays. The one who is here, right now, for the people who need me." A pause. "For you."
He clasps your arm. The grip is firm. Whatever he lost when the letter arrived, something else has taken its place. Something his own.
"Thank you. For not letting me face that alone."

choices.text: "I would not have it any other way."

ARC_THESS_1.title: THE STOLEN PAGES
prose: Thessaly is furious. You have never seen her furious before — she is usually so measured, so precise. But tonight her hands shake as she holds a torn binding.

"Someone has been reading my chronicle," she says. "Not just reading — copying. Pages are missing. The section about Commander Serath's defection. My notes on the garrison commander's order to abandon refugees. Everything I documented that might embarrass the wrong people."
