She paces. "I write the truth. That is all I do. I write what actually happened so that someone, somewhere, someday knows. And someone has decided that the truth is too dangerous to let stand."
Her jaw sets. "I am going to find out who."

choices.text: "I will help you find them."
"Be careful. Whoever did this has power."

ARC_THESS_2.title: THE OFFICER
prose: It takes three days to trace the theft. The culprit is a Solamnic officer named Ser Rendal — a political creature who has been compiling a dossier on knights he considers disloyal. Thessaly's chronicle was perfect raw material.

"He wants to use my words to purge people," Thessaly says. "Knights who showed mercy to refugees. Officers who questioned bad orders. Everyone who did the right thing instead of the procedurally correct thing." She looks sick.
Ser Rendal has already sent copies to three council members. The damage may already be done.

"I have to decide," Thessaly says. "Destroy my own copies — burn the chronicle — so he cannot use the originals to verify his excerpts. Or confront him publicly and pray that sunlight is still a disinfectant in this political climate."

choices.text: "Burn it. Protect the people in those pages."
"Confront him. The truth survives or it means nothing."
"Let me handle Rendal. Keep the chronicle safe."

ARC_THESS_3.title: THE COST OF TRUTH
prose: The fallout is quiet but real.

If the chronicle was burned, Thessaly is hollow. She stares at campfires differently now — as if remembering what she fed to the flames. "Three months of work," she says once. "Every conversation. Every observation. Gone because one man decided the truth was a weapon."

If Rendal was confronted, it became a political mess. His allies rallied. The council demanded Thessaly surrender her notes for review. She refused. Her name is now on a list of troublemakers — which, she notes drily, is exactly the kind of list Rendal was making.
Either way, the question sits at the centre of her craft like a crack in a foundation: *Is the truth always worth telling?*

choices.text: "The truth matters. Even when it costs."
"Sometimes protecting people matters more."

ARC_THESS_4.title: THE NEW SECTION
prose: Thessaly sits by the morning fire with a fresh quill and a blank book. She writes the date at the top of the first page.
"I started a new chronicle," she says without looking up. "Not a replacement. The old one documented what happened. This one will document what it meant."

She pauses. "I used to think being a chronicler meant recording facts. I was wrong. It means choosing which facts matter — and being honest about the choosing. Every historian is also an editor. The ones who pretend otherwise are the most dangerous."
She writes for a moment, then glances at you. Almost shy. "I put you in the first paragraph. I hope that is acceptable."
She returns to her work. The quill scratches steadily. Whatever Thessaly lost, she has rebuilt it into something more deliberate. More hers.

choices.text: "It is an honour."

ARC_SERATH_1.title: THE HUNTERS
prose: Serath wakes everyone before dawn. Their eyes are flat — the look of a soldier delivering bad news.
"Dragon Army scouts. Three of them, moving parallel to our route. They are not foraging. They are not lost." A pause. "They are hunting me."
They lay out the evidence: boot prints in a specific Dragon Army pattern, the burned campfire of a tracking unit, a signal mirror flash two nights ago.

"There is a bounty on my head. Five hundred gold pieces and a promotion for whoever brings me back. I told you this would happen." Their voice is controlled, but their hands are not. "I need you to understand what this means for all of you."

choices.text: "We protect our own. End of discussion."
"What are our tactical options?"

ARC_SERATH_2.title: THE DEAL
prose: One of the scouts is captured. A young soldier — barely older than you — with a Dragon Army tabard and terrified eyes.
Serath interrogates them. The scout offers a deal: they will report Serath dead in exchange for safe passage for a group of refugees trapped behind Dragon Army lines.
"It is a trick," Serath says. "Or it is not. If it is genuine, fifty people live. If it is a trap, they know our exact position and we are dead by morning."
The captured scout says nothing. They just look at Serath with an expression that might be guilt.

choices.text: "Refuse. We cannot risk the group on a gamble."
"Feed false information. Send them east while we go west."
"Let Serath decide. It is their life at stake."

ARC_SERATH_3.title: THE COST OF DEFECTION
prose: The hunters close in regardless. Two more patrols spotted in as many days. Serath barely sleeps.
On the third night, they come to you alone. "I have been thinking about turning myself in," they say. No preamble. No softening. "If I go back, they stop hunting. The rest of you are safe."

They stare into the dark. "I defected because I watched my unit burn a village. Women and children. The commander said it was strategic necessity. I said it was murder. They said the same thing." A long silence. "I ran. I have been running since. Perhaps it is time to stop."
There is something in their voice that is not fear. It is exhaustion. The kind that goes deeper than the body.

choices.text: "You are not going back. Not while I breathe."
"Turning yourself in would not save anyone. They want information."

ARC_SERATH_4.title: STANDING GROUND
prose: Serath stays.

Not because you convinced them with logic, or because the tactical situation changed, or because running was no longer an option. They stay because you asked them to. And in a life defined by orders — followed, refused, escaped — being asked is something new.

"I spent fifteen years in the Dragon Armies," they say at dawn, watching the first light break over the trees. "Fifteen years of following orders. Then one day I stopped. I thought that was the bravest thing I had ever done."
They look at you. "I was wrong. Staying is harder than running. It always has been."
They clasp your shoulder — a soldier's grip, brief and firm. "Thank you. For making this feel like something worth staying for."

choices.text: "The road ahead needs you. We all do."

ARC_MIRA_1.title: THE ONE SHE COULDN'T SAVE
prose: The traveller arrives at dusk, carried between two strangers. A young man, barely conscious, with wounds that tell a story of ambush and flight.

Mira works through the night. Yarrow for the bleeding. Willow bark for the fever. Comfrey poultices for the broken ribs. Everything she knows, every technique she has learned in a decade of frontier healing.

By midnight, she knows. You can see it in the way her hands slow. The wounds are too deep. Without clerical magic — without the divine healing that vanished three hundred years ago — there is nothing she can do.
She does not stop working. She just works quieter.
The traveller dies at dawn. His daughter, a girl of perhaps seven, sits outside the tent and does not cry.

choices.text: Stay with Mira.
Go to the child.

ARC_MIRA_2.title: THE CONVERSATION
prose: The child's name is Elara. She sits by the cold campfire and does not speak.
Mira kneels beside her. "I am going to tell you something that will not make you feel better," she says. "Your father was very badly hurt. I tried everything I know. It was not enough."
The girl looks at her. "Could the old healers have saved him? The ones from before?"
Mira's composure cracks. Just slightly — a tremor in the jaw, a brightness in the eyes. "Yes," she says. "Probably."
"Then why did the gods leave?"
Mira has no answer. She sits with the child in silence. After a while, the girl leans against her shoulder.

choices.text: Help Mira with the burial.
Try to find a caravan to take Elara to safety.
Search the area for rare healing herbs. (Survival DC 14)

ARC_MIRA_3.title: THE BREAKING POINT
prose: Three days after the burial, Mira stops healing.

Not dramatically. Not with announcement or tears. She simply does not reach for her herb pouch when someone cuts themselves on a branch. She does not check the bandages at rest stops. She sits apart and stares at nothing.
When asked, she says: "What is the point? Every person I save will face something I cannot fix. Every wound I close leads to a wound I cannot. I am holding back a tide with my hands."
It is the most frightening thing she has ever said, because she says it calmly. As a fact.

choices.text: "You saved dozens of people who would be dead without you."
"You are grieving. That is allowed."

ARC_MIRA_4.title: THE HEALER RETURNS
prose: A week later, someone in the company twists an ankle on loose rock. Without thinking, Mira is there. Hands moving before her mind catches up. Yarrow. Compress. Splint.
She finishes and sits back. Looks at her own hands as if surprised to find them working.

"I was twelve when I healed my first person," she says quietly. "A boy who fell from a tree. I packed the wound with moss because I did not know any better. It worked anyway." She almost smiles. "I did not choose to be a healer because I thought I could save everyone. I chose it because I could not bear to do nothing."
She picks up her herb pouch. Dusts it off. "In a world without gods, a healer who stops healing is just another person doing nothing. And there are enough of those."
She looks at you. "Thank you. For letting me break. And for being here when I put myself back together."

choices.text: "Always."

ARC_CAERON_1.title: THE ASHVALE SECRET
prose: Caeron receives a letter sealed with wax he recognises. His face goes white, then red, then carefully neutral.
"Lord Garath Penrose," he says. "An old ally of my family — or so I thought. He says he knows what really happened. The disgrace. The real reason the Ashvales fell."

He turns the letter over in his hands. "The official story is that my grandfather embezzled from the Order's treasury. That he betrayed the trust of the Crown. But Penrose says — he says it was a setup. That another family framed ours to seize our lands."
His voice is tight. "He says he has proof. Letters. Ledgers. Enough to clear the Ashvale name completely."
He does not look like a man who has been given good news. He looks like a man who has been given a bomb.

choices.text: "This is what you have been waiting for."
"Be careful. What does Penrose want in return?"

ARC_CAERON_2.title: THE PRICE OF A NAME
prose: The answer, of course, is something. Penrose wants Caeron to swear fealty to his political faction — a coalition of minor nobles positioning themselves against the Solamnic high command.

"He will clear our name," Caeron says. "Restore the Ashvale lands, the title, everything my family lost. All I have to do is become his political creature. Vote as he votes. Support his candidates. Be the grateful lord who owes everything to Garath Penrose."
He laughs, but there is no humour in it. "My grandfather was framed for selling his honour for gold. And now I am being asked to sell my honour for a name."
He looks at you. For all his careful composure, all his practised nobility, he looks very young.

choices.text: "Take the deal. Your family deserves restoration."
"Refuse. Your honour is not for sale."
"Find the proof independently. You do not need Penrose."

ARC_CAERON_3.title: THE MAN BEHIND THE NAME
prose: Whatever Caeron chose, the weight of it settles on him like armour.

If he took the deal, he writes letters late at night — political correspondence that makes his jaw tight and his handwriting sharp. He has his name back, or will soon. He is not sure it was worth the price.
If he refused, the Ashvale name remains disgraced. Penrose's letter goes unanswered. Caeron burns it, watches the wax seal melt, and says nothing for an hour.

If he chose independence, the road ahead is long and uncertain. Finding proof without Penrose means travelling to archives, bribing clerks, risking everything on fragments of evidence that may not exist.
In every case, Caeron is quieter. More real. The performative nobility has fallen away.

choices.text: "The name is not what makes you a lord."
"Whatever comes next, you face it with honour."

ARC_CAERON_4.title: THE LORD HIMSELF
prose: Caeron catches you watching the sunset from a ridge and joins you. He stands differently now — less performed, more present.

"Do you know what the Ashvale motto was?" he asks. "*Per Aspera ad Honorem.* Through hardship to honour." He shakes his head. "I always thought that meant the hardship was the price of honour. But I think it means the hardship IS the honour. The willingness to endure it."
He pulls the signet ring from his finger and looks at it. The Ashvale crest, still turned inward.
Then, slowly, he turns it outward.
"I am Lord Caeron Ashvale," he says. "Not because of lands or titles or political alliances. Because I chose to carry the name when it cost something to carry."
He puts the ring back on. "Thank you. For seeing the man before you saw the name."

choices.text: "The name is lucky to have you."

ARC_SERA_1.title: THE OUTPOST
prose: The ranger outpost appears through the trees like a ghost. Sera stops dead. Her face, usually so composed, goes completely blank.
"I know this place," she says. Nothing else. She walks through the broken door as if pulled by a thread.
Inside: two bunks, a cold hearth, a rack for bows that still holds one. Maps on the wall, yellowed and curling. A leather journal on the table, covered in dust.
Sera stands in the doorway and does not move. "I lived here," she says. "For eight months. With someone." A long pause. "Their name was Rowan."

choices.text: Wait for her to continue.
"What happened to Rowan?"

ARC_SERA_2.title: ROWAN'S JOURNAL
prose: Sera picks up the journal. Her hands are steady but her breathing is not.
"Rowan was a ranger. Like me. We patrolled together, shared watches, shared — everything." She opens the journal. "I left on a three-day scouting run. Standard work. Routine."
She turns pages. Her eyes move but you are not sure she is reading.
"A bandit group hit the outpost while I was gone. Rowan held them off alone for two days. By the time I came back —" She closes the journal. "I was four hours too late."
She holds the journal against her chest. "That is why I do not stay. That is why I leave. Because every time I stay, someone depends on me being there. And I am never there when it matters."

choices.text: "Read it with me. You do not have to carry this alone."
Give her space. Step outside.
"What happened was not your fault, Sera."

ARC_SERA_3.title: THE WALL
prose: Whether you stayed or stepped away, Sera eventually emerges. She looks like someone who has been crying without letting any tears fall.
"Rowan wrote about me in the journal," she says. "The last entry. Written while the bandits were outside." Her voice is carefully flat. "It says: *She will come back. She always comes back.*"
She leans against the outpost wall. "Rowan was wrong. I did come back. But not in time. And that is the thing about always coming back — it does not matter if you are too late."

She looks at the company, at the people she has been travelling with. At you. "Every day I stay with this group, I am making the same promise Rowan believed. That I will be here when it matters. And every day I am terrified that I will not be."

choices.text: "Then stay. And prove yourself right this time."
"No one expects you to save everyone. Just be here."

ARC_SERA_4.title: THE DECISION
prose: Sera is gone when you wake. Her bedroll is cold.
For one terrible hour, you think she has left. Done what she always does. Vanished before the staying gets too hard.
Then she appears from the tree line, two rabbits slung over her shoulder and a new-cut walking staff in her hand. She has been scouting the route ahead.

"I thought about leaving," she says, tossing the rabbits to the cook. "I packed my things. Got halfway to the ridge." She looks at you. "Then I turned around. Because Rowan was right. I do come back. I just need to learn to come back sooner."
She holds out the walking staff. "Made this for you. Good wood. Strong grain." She almost smiles. "I am not going anywhere. Not this time."

choices.text: Take the staff. "Welcome back."

ARC_VAEL_1.title: THE INDEPENDENT MISSION
prose: Vael has been selected for an independent scouting mission — a chance to prove their capability outside your shadow.
"Finally," they say, checking their equipment with practiced efficiency. "A real assignment. My own command, my own decisions, my own results." They glance at you. "No offence."
The mission is a forward reconnaissance of Dragon Army supply lines. Three days out, three days back. Important work. Dangerous work.
Vael is practically vibrating with anticipation. They have been waiting for this since the day they met you.

choices.text: "Be careful out there."
"Bring back something useful."

ARC_VAEL_2.title: THE RETURN
prose: Vael comes back on the fifth day instead of the sixth. They are limping. One arm is in a rough sling. Their armor has a dent that should not be there.
"Ambush," they say, sitting down hard. "Two Dragon Army outriders. I handled it." They wince. "Mostly."

The intelligence they gathered is good — supply line timing, garrison strength, a vulnerability in the eastern approach. But the mission went wrong. They were spotted. They had to fight their way out. Alone.
They will not look at you. The competitive mask is cracked and underneath it is something raw.

choices.text: "You survived a two-on-one ambush and brought back intel. That is a win."
Tend to their wounds without comment.
"You should not have gone alone."

ARC_VAEL_3.title: THE MASK SLIPS
prose: You find Vael sitting alone by the dying fire, staring at their injured arm.

"I have been competing with you since the day we met," they say. No edge. No sharpness. Just the fact. "Every training session, every assignment, every acknowledgement from Aldous — I measured it against you. And I was losing."

They flex their injured hand. "So I took the solo mission. My chance to be the one who came back with the crucial intelligence. The one everyone looked at." A bitter laugh. "And I almost died for it."
"Do you know what scared me? Not the ambush. Not the injuries. The thought that you would have handled it better. That even in my moment, I was still in your shadow."

choices.text: "It is not a competition. It never was."
"You are right. I probably would have. But that does not matter."

ARC_VAEL_4.title: THE RIVAL REFORGED
prose: Vael catches up to you on the march. Their arm is healing. Their pride is taking longer.
"I have been thinking," they say. "About what winning means. I spent so long trying to be better than you that I forgot to ask what I was better at."
They walk in silence for a moment. "I am a good scout. Fast, observant, thorough. That is what I brought back from the mission — not glory, not a dramatic victory. Information. The useful kind."

They give you a look — the old competitive edge, but transformed. "I am not going to stop trying to be the best. But I am done measuring best against you. I will measure it against the mission. Against what is needed."
A sharp salute — half-mocking, half-genuine, entirely Vael. "You are still insufferable, though. For the record."

choices.text: "And you are still the only one who keeps me honest. For the record."

ARC_TARIEL_1.title: THE FRAGMENT
prose: Tariel has been unusually quiet for three days — which, for Tariel, means they have only shared four unsolicited theories instead of the usual twelve.

Then they come to you with a piece of parchment, hands trembling with excitement. "I found this in the lining of a book I bought from a refugee," they say. "It is a fragment of a map. Pre-Cataclysm. And I know what it leads to."
They spread the parchment on a rock. The ink is faded but the landmarks are clear: a temple, hidden in the foothills east of the road.

"A Temple of Mishakal," Tariel breathes. "Goddess of healing. If it survived the Cataclysm — if any part of it still stands — it could contain proof that the gods are returning. Not rumours. Not signs and portents. Proof."
Their eyes are brighter than you have ever seen them. "It is a half-day detour. Maybe a full day. But if I am right..."

choices.text: "We go. Knowledge like this is worth the risk."
"Can we afford the delay? The Dragon Armies are close."

ARC_TARIEL_2.title: THE TEMPLE
prose: The temple is real. Half-buried under centuries of landslide and forest growth, but unmistakably a Temple of Mishakal. Broken columns. A shattered altar. A mosaic floor still showing the Blue Lady's symbol beneath the dirt.
Tariel moves through the ruins like a child in a dream. They touch every surface, read every carving, catalogue every detail in their cramped handwriting.

In the inner sanctum, they find it. A prayer niche, still intact. And on the altar — impossible, ridiculous, undeniable — a single flower. Growing from bare stone. In perfect bloom. In a ruin that has not seen sunlight in three hundred years.
Tariel stares at the flower for a very long time.
"This changes everything," they whisper. "This changes everything I thought I knew."

choices.text: "What does it mean?"
"The gods are returning. This is proof."

ARC_TARIEL_3.title: FAITH AND SCHOLARSHIP
prose: Tariel is different after the temple. They still take notes. They still theorize. But there is something underneath the scholarship now — something they cannot explain away with cross-references and citations.
"I have spent my entire life studying the gods as a historical phenomenon," they say. "The way one might study weather patterns or migratory routes. Observable. Measurable. Academic."
They look at their hands. "But that flower was not academic. It was not a historical artifact. It was alive. Growing. Present tense." A long pause. "I do not have a framework for present tense."
The tension between the scholar who needs evidence and the person who saw a miracle is visible. It sits in their shoulders, in the way they hold their books.

choices.text: "Faith is not the enemy of knowledge. It is the next question."
"Document it. Let the evidence speak for itself."

ARC_TARIEL_4.title: THE SCHOLAR AND THE FLOWER
prose: Tariel sits by the fire, a pressed flower between the pages of their newest book. They look at it often.

"I have a theory," they say. You wait. Tariel always has a theory. "I think the gods never left. I think they were always here, in the spaces between the observable world, waiting for someone to look in the right place."

They close the book. "I also think that is not a theory. I think it is faith. And I am still not entirely comfortable with that word." A small, crooked smile. "But I am less uncomfortable than I was."

They look at you with genuine warmth — rare, for someone who usually looks at people as if they are footnotes. "You came with me to the temple when you had every reason not to. You trusted my instinct over the evidence. That is — " They search for the word. "That is what the old texts call grace. I think I finally understand what they meant."

choices.text: "The world needs scholars who can also believe."

ARC_LYSANDER_1.title: THE BRIDGE
prose: The bridge at Garnet Ford is a stone span over a fast-moving river. Ordinary. Unremarkable. Lysander stops twenty paces from it and does not move.
His face is ash-grey. His hands, usually so steady, hang at his sides like dead weight.
"I have been here before," he says. It is the most words he has spoken in a day.

He does not elaborate. He does not need to. The way he looks at the bridge tells you everything: something happened here. Something that broke him. Something he has been carrying in silence since long before you met.

choices.text: "Take your time."
"What happened here, Lysander?"

ARC_LYSANDER_2.title: THE ORDER HE FOLLOWED
prose: Lysander speaks. Slowly. As if pulling each word from deep water.

"Two years ago. I was in a different unit. A border patrol. We were ordered to hold this bridge against a group of refugees trying to cross." He stares at the water. "The commander said they were potential Dragon Army sympathizers. That we could not risk the crossing."
"They were families. Old people. Children. They just wanted to get to the other side." His voice is flat. "We held the bridge. We followed orders. The refugees tried to cross the river on foot."
He is quiet for a long time.
"The river was faster than it looked. Seven people drowned. I stood on this bridge and watched."
Movement on the far bank. Another military unit is approaching — heading for the same bridge. Heading for the same mistake, perhaps.

choices.text: "We go to them. We warn them. Follow Lysander."
"Stay hidden. We do not know who they are."
"Lysander, stay here. I will handle this."

ARC_LYSANDER_3.title: THE GHOSTS
prose: Lysander crosses the bridge. Each step costs him something visible.
The unit on the other side is a Solamnic patrol. And behind them, a group of refugees being escorted — or herded — toward the crossing.

Lysander speaks to the patrol commander. Quietly. Directly. He tells them about the river current. About the rocks beneath the surface. About the seven people who drowned here because someone decided orders mattered more than lives.
The patrol commander listens. Then looks at the river. Then back at Lysander.
"We will find another crossing," the commander says. And they do.
Lysander stands on the bridge as the refugees pass. Some of them look at him. He does not look away.

choices.text: Stand with him on the bridge.
"You just saved them, Lysander."

ARC_LYSANDER_4.title: THE WEIGHT SHARED
prose: That night, Lysander speaks more than he has in the entire journey.

"I carried that bridge for two years," he says. "The faces. The sound of the water. I thought if I was quiet enough, if I helped enough, if I carried enough for other people, it would balance out." He shakes his head. "It does not work that way."
"What works is going back. Standing on the bridge. Making a different choice." He looks at his hands. "I could not save the people who drowned. But I could save the ones who were about to."
He is quiet again — but it is a different kind of quiet. Not the silence of someone hiding. The silence of someone who has finally said the thing they needed to say.
"Thank you," he says. "For not making me cross that bridge alone."
He hands you a water skin. A small, practical kindness. The kind he has always been best at.

choices.text: "You never have to cross alone again."

CONFRONT_ALDRIC.title: ALDRIC CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Aldric is waiting for you. Arms crossed. Jaw set. The easy grin is gone.

"We need to talk," he says. "I have followed you through every decision, every fork in the road, every choice that made my stomach turn. And I kept quiet. Because that is what a good squire does — loyalty, obedience, the whole Crown package."

He steps closer. "But loyalty is not the same as blindness. You have been making choices that would shame the Order. Selfish choices. Choices that put your interests above the people we swore to protect."
"I joined you because I believed you were the kind of knight worth following. Was I wrong?"

choices.text: "You are right. I have lost my way. I will do better."
"I made the best choices I could. You do not have to agree."
"Then leave. I will not force anyone to follow me."

CONFRONT_THESS.title: THESSALY CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Thessaly closes her chronicle with a sharp snap. She does not look at you. She looks through you.

"I cannot write this anymore," she says. "I am a chronicler. I document truth. But you — you lie. You hide things. You reshape what happened to suit yourself, and then you expect me to record your version as if it were fact."

Her voice is precise and devastating. "I have watched you deceive people who trusted you. I have watched you make promises you had no intention of keeping. And every time, I wrote it down, thinking perhaps the record itself would be enough."
"It is not enough. I will not be your court historian, polishing your legacy while you tarnish it."

choices.text: "You are right. The truth matters, and I have not honoured it."
"The truth is more complicated than your chronicle allows."
"Then stop writing. I never asked for a biographer."

CONFRONT_SERATH.title: SERATH CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Serath finds you away from the fire. Their posture is military — rigid, controlled, dangerous.
"I need you to listen carefully," they say. "I have served under three Dragon Army commanders. I know what bad leadership looks like. And you are beginning to resemble it."

Their voice is ice. "Reckless decisions. No regard for the people following you. Charging into situations without gathering intelligence, without considering the cost to others. I defected from an army that treated soldiers as expendable. I did not do so to follow another commander who does the same."
"Change your approach, or I walk. I have survived worse partings than this."

choices.text: "I hear you. I will be more careful with people's lives."
"War requires hard choices. Not every risk can be avoided."
"Then walk. I will not beg a defector to stay."

CONFRONT_MIRA.title: MIRA CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Mira is packing her herb pouch. Not reorganizing it. Packing it. As if to leave.

"I am a healer," she says quietly. "Do you understand what that means? It means every life matters. Every single one. Not as a resource to be spent or a calculation to be weighed — as a life. With a name and a history and someone who will grieve when it ends."
She ties the pouch shut. "You do not see that. You look at people and see tools. Means to an end. And when the end is served, you discard them without a second thought."
Her eyes are bright with something between anger and grief. "I cannot heal bodies while watching you wound souls. It is killing something inside me."

choices.text: "You are right. I have been callous, and I am sorry."
"I make hard choices so that more people live. That IS compassion."
"I understand. I will not stop you from leaving."

CONFRONT_CAERON.title: CAERON CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Caeron stands before you with the careful posture of a man delivering a formal address. But his voice shakes.
"I came on this journey to reclaim my family's honour," he says. "To prove that the Ashvale name still means something. That nobility is more than a title — it is a way of being."

He straightens. "And you treat honour like a joke. You mock the principles that people like me are fighting to embody. Every time you cut a corner, every time you choose expedience over principle, you make the world a little more like the one that destroyed my family."
"I will not watch you tear down what I am trying to rebuild."

choices.text: "You are right. Honour is not a joke, and I have treated it as one."
"Honour means nothing if you cannot survive to uphold it."
"Go find your honour elsewhere, then. I have a war to fight."

CONFRONT_SERA.title: SERA CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Sera catches your arm at the edge of camp. Her grip is iron. Her eyes are furious.
"I stayed," she says. "Against every instinct I have, against every lesson I have ever learned, I stayed. Because you made me think it was worth it."

She releases your arm. "But you do not let people be who they are. You control. You direct. You decide for others what they should want and how they should act, and when they push back, you bulldoze them."

"I am a ranger. I live by my own judgement. That is not something I gave up when I joined this group, and it is not something you get to take from me. I have lost one home to someone else's decisions. I will not lose another."

choices.text: "You are right. I have been controlling, and I am sorry."
"I lead because someone has to. But I hear you."
"Then go. The door was never locked."

CONFRONT_VAEL.title: VAEL CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Vael blocks your path. Not aggressively — precisely. The way you block a training partner in the yard.
"I came here to become a knight," they say. "The best knight. I have given everything to this — every hour of training, every missed meal, every night spent studying tactics instead of sleeping."

Their jaw is tight. "And you treat it like it is nothing. Like the work does not matter. Like the oath is just words and the training is just exercise and the whole thing is a game you can play without caring about the outcome."
"I do not care if you are better than me. I care that you do not even try. That is what I cannot stand."

choices.text: "You deserve better from me. I will take this seriously."
"I take this more seriously than you know. We simply disagree on method."
"If I am not meeting your standards, find someone who does."

CONFRONT_TARIEL.title: TARIEL CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Tariel approaches you with a book closed over one finger — marking their place, as always. But they are not reading.

"I have a concern," they say. The academic phrasing does not disguise the anger beneath it. "You have a pattern of ignoring information. Of dismissing intelligence, disregarding evidence, and charging forward on instinct alone."

They adjust their spectacles. "I understand that scholarship is not everyone's strength. But what you are doing is not merely anti-intellectual — it is dangerous. You make decisions without information. You discard counsel. You treat knowledge as an inconvenience rather than a tool."
"I joined this company because I believed my skills would be valued. I was wrong. You do not want a scholar. You want an audience."

choices.text: "You are right. I have not been listening, and that stops now."
"There are times when instinct matters more than information."
"If you feel undervalued, I understand. I will not hold you here."

CONFRONT_LYSANDER.title: LYSANDER CONFRONTS YOU
prose: Lysander is not one for speeches. So when he stands in front of you and speaks, the weight of it is enormous.
"You break promises," he says. Four words. Quiet. Final.
He waits. Lets the silence do the work.

"I have followed people who break promises before. I watched them abandon the people who trusted them. I watched them choose their own survival over everyone else's. I stood on a bridge while people drowned because a man who broke promises gave an order."
"I will not do that again. Not for anyone. Not even for you."

choices.text: "I hear you. A promise is a promise, and I will keep mine."
"Sometimes circumstances change. But I understand your line."
"I understand. Walk your own road, Lysander."

GIFT_ALDRIC.title: ALDRIC'S GIFT
prose: Aldric pulls you aside after the morning drills. He has that look — the one he gets when he has been working up to something for days.
"I want to show you something," he says. "My brother taught me this before — before everything." He does not finish the sentence. He does not need to.
He positions you in a grappling stance. "Lock the elbow like this. See? You control the shoulder through the elbow. Most people fight the arm. You fight the joint."
He walks you through it three times. Patient. Precise. A different Aldric from the one who charges into every fight.

"My brother said this hold saved his life twice. I am giving it to you because — " He stops. Starts again. "Because you are the person I trust most in the world. And I want you to survive whatever comes next."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Aldric teaches you a grappling technique. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Athletics checks.

choices.text: "Thank you, Aldric. Truly."

GIFT_THESS.title: THESSALY'S GIFT
prose: Thessaly sits beside you with her chronicle open to a complex web of annotations. She has been working on something for days.
"I want to teach you how I do this," she says. "Not the writing — the thinking. The way I verify what actually happened when everyone has a different version of events."
She traces a thread through her notes. "Three sources minimum. If two agree and one contradicts, the contradiction is usually the truth. People lie in the same direction. Truth is the outlier."
She spends an hour walking you through her method — cross-referencing, source evaluation, the art of reading between the lines. It is meticulous, brilliant work.

"Most people think history is about the past," she says, closing the book. "It is not. It is about learning to see what is really in front of you. I am giving you that skill because you deserve to see clearly."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Thessaly teaches you her method of analysis. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to History checks.

choices.text: "I will use it well."

GIFT_SERATH.title: SERATH'S GIFT
prose: Serath finds you at dusk, when the light makes expressions harder to read. They are smiling, which is rare.
"Sit," they say. "I am going to teach you something they taught me in officer training. Something useful."

They position you facing them. "Watch the hands, not the face. The face lies first. The hands forget to." They demonstrate — palms open, then closed, then touching the neck. "When someone touches their neck while speaking, they are deciding how much to tell you. When they touch their face, they have already decided to lie."
For an hour, they drill you on micro-expressions, verbal cadence, the subtle tells that betray deception. It is chilling, precise, effective.
"I spent fifteen years reading people for the Dragon Armies," Serath says. "That skill is not good or evil. It is a tool. In your hands, I trust it will be used to protect people, not exploit them."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Serath teaches you interrogation techniques. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Insight checks.

choices.text: "I will not forget this. Or your trust."

GIFT_MIRA.title: MIRA'S GIFT
prose: Mira leads you to a meadow at dawn, when the dew is still on the herbs. She has her gathering basket and a quiet, purposeful expression.

"Yarrow stops bleeding," she says, cutting a stem. "Willow bark dulls pain. Comfrey knits bone." She places each in your hands as she names them. "That is everything you need to know to save someone's life on the road. Everything else is refinement."

She teaches you to identify each herb by leaf shape, by smell, by the way they grow. She shows you how to make a poultice in the field, how to set a bone without tools, how to clean a wound when all you have is river water and hope.
"I cannot teach you everything I know," she says. "That took ten years of mistakes. But I can teach you enough to keep someone alive until a real healer arrives."
She touches your hand. "In a world without gods, every person who can heal matters. I am making you one more."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Mira teaches you field herbalism. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Medicine checks.

choices.text: "Thank you, Mira. For everything."

GIFT_CAERON.title: CAERON'S GIFT
prose: Caeron invites you to walk with him. He is more relaxed than usual — the practiced formality softened by genuine comfort.
"I want to teach you something my mother taught me," he says. "Before the disgrace, when we still had a court, she was the finest speaker in Solamnic society. Not the loudest — the most effective."
He stops walking. "Never raise your voice. The moment you shout, you have lost the room. Speak quieter. Make them lean in. The one who controls the silence controls the conversation."
He coaches you through exercises — how to pause for effect, how to lower your voice at the crucial moment, how to let silence do the work that words cannot.
"Rhetoric is not manipulation," he says. "It is the art of making truth sound as important as it actually is. Most people have good ideas and terrible delivery. I am fixing your delivery."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Caeron teaches you noble rhetoric. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Persuasion checks.

choices.text: "Your mother would be proud."

GIFT_SERA.title: SERA'S GIFT
prose: Sera wakes you before dawn with a hand on your shoulder and a finger to her lips. She leads you into the forest.

For three hours, she teaches you to see. Not look — see. The bent grass that shows which way a deer went. The moss patterns that tell you where water flows underground. The bird calls that change when predators are near.
"Moss grows on the wet side, not the north side," she says. "Birds fly toward water at dawn. And if you can hear a stream, you are already too close to the bank — floods come fast."
She shows you how to build a shelter from deadfall in fifteen minutes. How to start a fire in rain. How to read the sky for weather two days out.
"The forest is not dangerous," she says. "Ignorance is dangerous. The forest is just the forest. Once you learn to read it, it is the safest place in the world."
She gives you a rare, genuine smile. "Now it is your safe place too."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Sera teaches you wilderness craft. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Survival checks.

choices.text: "I will carry this with me always."

GIFT_VAEL.title: VAEL'S GIFT
prose: Vael challenges you to a staring contest. You think they are joking. They are not.
"Command presence," they say. "The thing that makes a room go quiet when you walk in. It is not size. It is not volume. It is certainty."

They position you in a doorway. "Stand still. Do not fidget. Look at the bridge of their nose, not their eyes — it unsettles them without them knowing why. And never threaten. Threats are for people who are not sure they will follow through."

They drill you for an hour. Posture, eye contact, the art of controlled silence. It is the same competitive intensity they bring to everything, but directed at making you better instead of beating you.

"I spent my whole life learning how to be the most intimidating person in the room," Vael says. "Turns out the real skill is knowing when to use it and when to put it away." They grin. "You already know when. I am just giving you the how."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Vael teaches you command presence. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Intimidation checks.

choices.text: "I will not forget this. Or you."

GIFT_TARIEL.title: TARIEL'S GIFT
prose: Tariel has arranged a collection of stones on a flat rock. Some of them glow faintly in the firelight. You are fairly sure stones should not do that.

"Magic leaves a resonance," they say, holding one up. "Like a bell that was struck and stopped — the sound is gone but the vibration remains in the metal. Learn to feel it in old stone. It will tell you what was here before."

They teach you to detect magical residue — the faint warmth of old enchantments, the way pre-Cataclysm artifacts feel slightly too heavy for their size, the hum that lingers in places where divine magic was once practiced.
"This is not spellcasting," they clarify. "This is literacy. The magical equivalent of learning to read. The world is covered in writing that most people cannot see. I am teaching you the alphabet."
They press the warmest stone into your palm. "Keep this. When you feel something similar in the wild, trust the feeling. It means something was here. Something important."

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Tariel teaches you to detect magical resonance. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Arcana checks.

choices.text: "Thank you, Tariel. For showing me how to see."

GIFT_LYSANDER.title: LYSANDER'S GIFT
prose: Lysander taps your shoulder at midnight. He does not speak. He just gestures: follow me.
In the dark, away from the fire, he teaches you to disappear.
"Heel first, then roll to the toe," he murmurs. "Match your breathing to the wind. And never look directly at someone you are hiding from — they feel it. I do not know why. They just do."

He is a patient teacher — more patient than you expected from someone so taciturn. He corrects your footwork with gentle touches rather than words. He demonstrates each movement three times, then watches you do it until you get it right.

"I learned this from necessity," he says, the most he has ever explained about his past. "Staying alive meant staying unseen. I am not proud of why I learned it. But the skill itself is clean. Use it well."
He hands you a water skin. It is the same gesture he makes every morning. But tonight it feels different. Heavier. More deliberate. Like he is handing you everything he has.

boxes.text: **BOND GIFT** — Lysander teaches you silent movement. You gain a permanent +1 bonus to Stealth checks.

choices.text: "Thank you, Lysander. For trusting me with this."

CEREMONY_VIGIL.title: THE VIGIL
prose: The chapel is cold. Ancient stone, older than the fortress itself. The only light comes from seven candles arranged in the shape of the platinum dragon's constellation.
You kneel alone, your sword laid across your palms. The blade catches the candlelight — each nick and scratch a story you remember. Every battle. Every choice.
The night stretches. Hours pass like water through cupped hands.
You think of the road that brought you here. The companions who walked it with you. The moments when you could have turned back — but didn't.
Somewhere in the deep watches of the night, you hear footsteps. A priest enters, checks the candles, and leaves without speaking. Even he knows this silence is not his to break.
By the time dawn touches the eastern windows, you have made your peace with what comes next.

boxes.text: **THE VIGIL** — You have spent the night in prayer and reflection. At dawn, you will face the Hall of High Justice.

choices.text: Rise. It is time.

CEREMONY_TIEBREAKER_ROSE_SWORD.title: TWO PATHS
prose: Before you can begin your vigil, two figures enter the antechamber. They wear the symbols of different orders — the Rose and the Sword — and both have come for you.
Lady Elara of the Rose speaks first. She is old, her hands spotted with age, but her eyes hold the sharp clarity of a scholar who has read every text worth reading.

"The Rose remembers," she says softly. "When others saw only a battle to win, you saw people to save. Knowledge without compassion is cruelty; courage without wisdom is recklessness. The Rose needs knights who understand this."
Ser Garrett of the Sword steps forward. His face is a map of scars, his armor dented from a hundred battles. But his voice is gentle.

"I've watched you fight," he says. "Not for glory — for the ones who couldn't fight for themselves. The Sword isn't about killing. It's about standing between darkness and those it would devour. That's you."
Both watch you. Waiting.

choices.text: "I choose the Rose. Wisdom guides my blade."
"I choose the Sword. My strength for the weak."

CEREMONY_TIEBREAKER_ROSE_CROWN.title: TWO PATHS
prose: Before you can begin your vigil, two figures enter the antechamber. They wear the symbols of different orders — the Rose and the Crown — and both have come for you.
Lady Elara of the Rose speaks first. She is old, her hands spotted with age, but her eyes hold the sharp clarity of a scholar who has read every text worth reading.

"The Rose remembers," she says softly. "When others saw only a battle to win, you saw people to save. Knowledge without compassion is cruelty; courage without wisdom is recklessness. The Rose needs knights who understand this."
Dame Isolde of the Crown steps forward. Her bearing is regal, her voice measured — every word chosen with care.

"Order is not oppression," she says. "It is the promise that tomorrow will come. You've shown you understand that law serves the people, not the other way around. The Crown needs knights who can lead, not just follow."
Both watch you. Waiting.

choices.text: "I choose the Rose. Wisdom guides my blade."
"I choose the Crown. Law is my guide."

CEREMONY_TIEBREAKER_SWORD_CROWN.title: TWO PATHS
prose: Before you can begin your vigil, two figures enter the antechamber. They wear the symbols of different orders — the Sword and the Crown — and both have come for you.
Ser Garrett of the Sword speaks first. His face is a map of scars, his armor dented from a hundred battles. But his voice is gentle.

"I've watched you fight," he says. "Not for glory — for the ones who couldn't fight for themselves. The Sword isn't about killing. It's about standing between darkness and those it would devour. That's you."
Dame Isolde of the Crown steps forward. Her bearing is regal, her voice measured — every word chosen with care.

"Order is not oppression," she says. "It is the promise that tomorrow will come. You've shown you understand that law serves the people, not the other way around. The Crown needs knights who can lead, not just follow."
Both watch you. Waiting.

choices.text: "I choose the Sword. My strength for the weak."
"I choose the Crown. Law is my guide."

CEREMONY_TIEBREAKER_ALL.title: THREE PATHS
prose: Before you can begin your vigil, three figures enter the antechamber. Each wears the symbol of a different order — Rose, Sword, Crown — and all three have come for you.
Lady Elara of the Rose speaks first. "The Rose remembers. When others saw only a battle to win, you saw people to save. Wisdom and compassion — the Rose needs knights who understand this."
Ser Garrett of the Sword steps forward. "I've watched you fight. Not for glory — for the ones who couldn't fight for themselves. The Sword needs knights who stand between darkness and the innocent."
Dame Isolde of the Crown inclines her head. "Order is the promise that tomorrow will come. The Crown needs knights who can lead, who understand that law serves the people."
Three paths. Three orders. Each has chosen you. Now you must choose.

choices.text: "I choose the Rose. Wisdom guides my blade."
"I choose the Sword. My strength for the weak."
"I choose the Crown. Law is my guide."

CEREMONY_HALL.title: THE HALL OF HIGH JUSTICE
prose: Dawn light floods the Hall of High Justice. Ancient banners hang from rafters older than memory — the platinum dragon of Paladine, the kingfisher of Brightholm, the rose and sword and crown of the three orders.
The Knights of Solamnia stand in witness. Weathered faces. Gleaming armor. The weight of centuries in their silence.
At the far end of the hall, Commander Serath waits. His blade is drawn — old steel, notched from battles you've only heard sung. The same blade that knighted him, decades ago.
Your companions stand among the witnesses. You see their faces — some proud, some afraid for you, some already composing how they'll tell this story later.
The hall falls silent. Even the wind outside seems to hold its breath.
"Approach," Serath commands.

choices.text: Walk forward. This is the moment you were made for.

CEREMONY_OATHS.title: THE THREE OATHS
prose: You kneel before Commander Serath. The stone is cold through your trousers. The hall holds its breath.

"You have walked the path of the Squire," Serath intones. His voice fills the hall without shouting — a trick of the acoustics, or of decades spent commanding men. "You have learned the Measure. You have been tested in battle and in choice."
He looks down at you. In this light, you can see every year he's carried.
"Now you will speak the Oaths. And if you speak them true, you will rise as something more than you were."
The hall waits.

boxes.text: **THE OATHS** — You will now speak the sacred words of Solamnic knighthood. [KNIGHTING_OATHS]

choices.text: Speak the Oath of the Rose: "Est Sularus oth Mithas — My honor is my life."
Speak the Oath of the Sword: "Est Solarus oth Kadran — My strength for the weak."
Speak the Oath of the Crown: "Est Draconis oth Paladine — The law is my guide."

CEREMONY_DUBBING.title: THE DUBBING
prose: Your voice echoes off the ancient stone. The oath hangs in the air — words spoken by ten thousand knights before you, each one meaning them as much as you do now.
Serath nods. Once. The smallest gesture, carrying the largest weight.
He raises his blade. The notched steel catches the dawn light.
The flat of the blade touches your left shoulder. Cold. Heavy. Real.
Then your right.
"By the authority of the High Clerist," Serath intones. "By the Measure that binds us. By the witness of Paladine himself."
The blade lifts. Serath's eyes meet yours.
"Rise, Ser. Knight of the Order."

boxes.text: **KNIGHTED** — You are now a Knight of Solamnia. [APPLY_KNIGHTING]

choices.text: Rise.

CEREMONY_SIGIL.title: THE SIGIL
prose: You stand. For the first time, you stand as a knight.
Serath reaches into his cloak and withdraws something that catches the light — a medallion of pure Solamnic silver, the symbol of your order etched deep into its face.
He places it in your hands. It is heavier than it looks. Heavier than silver should be.

"This is your proof," Serath says quietly, for you alone. "Your bond. Without it, you are merely someone claiming to be a Knight of Solamnia. With it, you carry the weight of the Oath, the Measure, and every knight who fell upholding both."
He releases the medallion into your hands.
"Carry it always. It will remind you of what you swore today. And what that oath will cost you, before the end."

choices.text: Close your fingers around the sigil. Feel its weight.

CEREMONY_HALL_RISES.title: THE HALL RISES
prose: The knights stamp their feet.
The sound begins as a rumble — boots on ancient stone — and builds into thunder. A rhythm that shakes dust from the banners. That makes the torch flames dance.
They are cheering. For you.
Through the noise, you see your companions. Their faces tell you everything words cannot.
[COMPANION_REACTIONS]
The stamping fades. The silence that follows is different from the silence before. Warmer. You belong here now.
Serath's hand lands on your shoulder. Just for a moment.
"Welcome, Ser. Now the real work begins."

choices.text: Turn to face your future as a Knight of Solamnia.

CEREMONY_UNPROVEN.title: THE UNPROVEN PATH
prose: Dawn light floods the Hall of High Justice. The Knights of Solamnia stand in witness. Commander Serath waits at the far end, his blade drawn.
But something is wrong. You can feel it in the air.
When you kneel before Serath, he studies you for a long moment. His eyes are not unkind — but they are not merciful either.
"You have served," he says finally. "You have fought. You have walked the road with us."
He sheathes his blade. The sound echoes in the suddenly silent hall.
"But the Measure is clear. A knight must show which path they walk. Rose, Sword, or Crown. You have not."
The words hit harder than any blow.
"You remain a Squire. This is not punishment—" He pauses. Chooses his words. "It is opportunity. The trials ahead will test you. Show us who you truly are, and the Order will welcome you."
He places a hand on your shoulder. "Until then, Squire. Serve well."

boxes.text: **UNPROVEN** — You remain a Squire. In the campaigns ahead, you may yet prove yourself worthy of knighthood. [SET_FLAG:unproven_squire]

choices.text: Accept this with grace. You will prove yourself.
Bow your head. The shame is heavy, but you will carry it.

TEMPT_SUPPLIES.title: THE ABANDONED LARDER
prose: *An abandoned farmstead — Late Afternoon*

The family left in a hurry. The door hangs open. Inside: salted meat, dried fruit, a half-wheel of cheese, travel bread wrapped in cloth. Enough to feed your group for three days. Enough to feed a refugee family for a week.
No one would know. The family is gone — fled south, or dead, or somewhere between. These supplies will spoil if no one takes them. The practical thing is clear.
But you are not here to be practical. You are here to be a knight. And knights do not take what is not offered, even from the abandoned. Even when it makes sense.
Aldric looks at the food. Looks at you. Says nothing. The choice is yours.

boxes.text:
**MORAL CHOICE**
The Measure does not explicitly forbid taking abandoned supplies. But the spirit of the Oath asks: if the family returns, what will they find? If refugees pass this way, what will sustain them?

choices.text: Take the supplies. The column needs them and the family is gone. This is war.
Leave coin on the table — more than the food is worth. Take only what you need.
Leave everything. Write a note: 'Solamnic patrol passed. Supplies untouched. Est Sularus.'

TEMPT_CREDIT.title: THE STOLEN VICTORY
prose: *Field Camp — After the Skirmish*

The patrol is reporting to Caeron. The flanking manoeuvre that won the skirmish — the one that turned a losing fight into a rout — Caeron thinks it was your idea. He says so, in front of everyone. 'Quick thinking, squire. That kind of tactical instinct is exactly what the Order needs.'

But it wasn't your idea. It was Aldric's. He suggested it in the chaos, you relayed it, and somehow in the telling it became yours. Aldric is standing three feet away. He heard Caeron's praise. His face is carefully blank.
No one would contradict you. Aldric certainly won't — he doesn't seek credit, never has. Caeron's good opinion is valuable. The Order notices things like this.
Do you take what isn't yours?

choices.text: Accept the credit. It's a small thing, and Aldric doesn't seem to mind.
'The flanking was Aldric's idea, my lord. I only passed it on.'

TEMPT_PRISONER.title: THE WOUNDED SCOUT
prose: *South Road — Dawn*

The Dragon Army scout lies against a tree, holding his stomach. Your blade did that. He is young — maybe younger than you — and he is dying slowly. His unit is gone, fled when the ambush turned. No one will come for him.
He has information. You can see it in his eyes — he knows things. Patrol routes. Supply lines. Maybe even where the main force is heading. Information that could save lives. Solamnic lives.

The Measure is clear: prisoners are to be treated with honour, offered quarter, given medical care if possible. But the Measure was written before the Dragon Armies. Before draconians. Before the enemy stopped taking prisoners themselves.
He watches you approach. Fear and defiance and something that might be hope.
What do you do with a dying enemy who knows things you need to know?

boxes.text:
**GRAVE CHOICE**
The information could save lives. The method of obtaining it will stain yours.

choices.text: Heal him. Offer quarter. Ask nothing — if he wants to talk, he will. The Measure is the Measure.
'Tell me what you know and I'll make the pain stop.' Press the wound.
Walk away. Let nature take its course. You didn't kill him — the wound did.

TEMPT_PRISONER_INFO.title: WHAT HE TELLS YOU
prose: *South Road — Moments Later*
He talks. They always talk. The Dragon Army camp is two days east. The supply wagons come every third day. There's a commander — a Highlord's lieutenant — who rides with a small guard.
Good intelligence. Actionable intelligence. Caeron will want to know.
When he's done talking, the scout looks at you with something that isn't fear anymore. It's recognition. He knows what you are now. So do you.
He dies an hour later. You could have healed him after. You didn't.
Aldric won't meet your eyes for the rest of the day.

boxes.text:
**CONSEQUENCE**
You have intelligence that may save lives. You have also crossed a line the Measure drew very clearly. Some companions will remember this.

choices.text: Report the intelligence to Caeron. Do not mention how you obtained it.

RETURN_TO_MARCH.title: THE MARCH CONTINUES
prose: The column moves on. The road stretches south. Whatever you chose back there — it's done now. The weight of it settles into the miles like everything else.

choices.text: Continue.

DEPTH_ALDRIC_FIRE.title: BY THE FIRE
prose: *Camp — Late Watch*
Everyone else is asleep. Aldric sits by the dying fire, staring at his hands. When he notices you watching, he doesn't look away.

'My father was a knight,' he says quietly. 'Order of the Sword. Died at the Battle of Fallow Fields when I was eight.' He turns his palm over, studying the calluses. 'I remember thinking — if he was such a great knight, why didn't he come home?'
The fire crackles. Somewhere in the darkness, an owl calls.

'Now I understand. He didn't *choose* not to come home. He chose something else *instead* of coming home. The oath. The cause. The people who needed him more than we did.' Aldric finally looks at you. 'I used to hate him for that. Now I'm terrified I'm becoming him.'

choices.text: 'The fact that you're scared means you're not him. He probably never doubted.'
'Maybe becoming him isn't the worst thing. He sounds like a good man.'
Say nothing. Just sit with him until dawn.

DEPTH_THESS_LOCKET.title: THE LOCKET
prose: *Camp — Before Dawn*
You find Thessaly holding her locket, thumb tracing the silver edge. She doesn't startle when you approach — she heard you coming. She always does.

'It was my mother's.' She doesn't open it. 'She gave it to me the night before the Dragon Armies came to our village. Told me to run and not look back.' Her voice is steady, reciting facts. 'I was eleven. I ran. I didn't look back.'

She finally meets your eyes. 'I've never opened it. Because if I open it and see her face, then she's real. And if she's real, then I left her. As long as the locket stays closed, she's just... a story I tell myself.'
Her fingers tighten around the silver.
'Some nights I almost open it. Tonight is one of those nights.'

choices.text: 'You don't have to open it tonight. Or ever. It's yours to decide.'
'If you want to open it, I'll be here. You won't be alone when you see her.'
'Running was the right choice. She wanted you to live. You did.'

DEPTH_MIRA_HEALING.title: THE HEALER'S HANDS
prose: *Camp — Dusk*
Mira is grinding herbs by firelight, her movements practiced and sure. But her hands are shaking.

'I ran out,' she says without looking up. 'During the last fight. I had one poultice left and two people bleeding. I chose who to save.' She crushes the herbs harder than necessary. 'The other one died calling for his mother.'
She sets down the mortar, finally still.

'Everyone thinks healers are gentle. That we're soft. They don't understand — we make decisions every day that haunt us every night. Who lives. Who dies. Who gets the last bandage, the last herb, the last prayer.'
Her dark eyes find yours.
'I'm very good at what I do. That's not a boast. It's an explanation for why I haven't broken yet.'

choices.text: 'You saved who you could. The one who died — that's not on you. That's on the war.'
'How do you choose? When you have to.'
Help her grind the herbs. Some things don't need words.

DEPTH_SERATH_COMMAND.title: THE WEIGHT OF COMMAND
prose: *Camp — Midnight*
Commander Serath stands at the edge of camp, watching the northern horizon. There's smoke there. There's always smoke now.

'I lost forty-three knights at the Neraka crossing,' he says when you join him. His voice is perfectly level. 'Forty-three good people who followed my orders into an ambush I should have seen coming. The Dragon Army commander sent me their tabards. Folded neatly. With a note thanking me for my contribution to their cause.'
He's silent for a long moment.
'I keep those tabards in my pack. Every single one. People think it's memorial. Respect for the fallen.' His jaw tightens. 'It's penance. Weight. So I never forget the cost of a wrong decision.'
He finally looks at you.
'Command is not about being right. It's about being responsible when you're wrong. Remember that.'

choices.text: 'They didn't die because of you. They died because of the enemy. There's a difference.'
'Carrying the weight is what makes you worth following. Lesser commanders forget.'
'What would you have done differently? If you could go back.'

DIVINE_PALADINE_GUIDANCE.title: A LIGHT IN DARKNESS
prose: *The March — Twilight*
You are walking when the light changes. Not sunset — something else. A quality of gold in the air that shouldn't exist. The others don't seem to notice.
And then you *hear* it. Not words. Not exactly. A presence at the edge of thought, vast and sorrowful and kind.
**'Child of the Oath.'**
The voice is everywhere and nowhere. You feel it in your bones, in the steel of your weapon, in the oath you swore.
**'The darkness rises. But you carry a light you do not yet understand. Choose well. Choose true. The Measure is not just law — it is the shape of what you might become.'**
Then it fades. The twilight returns to normal. Your companions look at you strangely — you've stopped walking. Tears are running down your face and you don't know why.
Something has changed. You feel it in your soul.

boxes.text:
**DIVINE CONTACT — PALADINE**
The Platinum Dragon speaks to those who carry the light. This is the first true divine contact in three hundred years. You are not alone.

choices.text: Speak of it to your companions. This cannot be kept secret.
Hold it close. Some truths are too fragile to speak aloud yet.

DIVINE_MISHAKAL_VISION.title: THE HEALER'S GIFT
prose: *Camp — Before Dawn*
You wake from a dream of blue light. A woman's face, beautiful beyond mortal measure, with eyes that held all the sorrows of the world and all its hopes in equal measure.
**'Healer. Protector. The wounds you cannot see are deeper than the ones you can.'**

Your hands are warm. Not hot — warm like sunlight through glass. When you look at Mira sleeping nearby, you can *see* the exhaustion in her like a grey shadow. When you look at Aldric, you see the grief he carries like a stone in his chest.
The warmth fades. But the sight remains. Just for a moment — just long enough to understand.
Healing is not just about bodies. It never was.
Mishakal, the Blue Lady, goddess of healing. Her constellation has been missing from the sky for three centuries. But her light just touched you. The gods are returning.

boxes.text:
**DIVINE VISION — MISHAKAL**
The Blue Lady has granted you sight beyond sight. Your Lay on Hands can now also remove one level of exhaustion per use (costs 5 points from pool).

choices.text: Use the gift. Touch Mira's shoulder as she sleeps. Let her rest deeply.
Sit with the vision until dawn. Let the gift settle before you use it.

DIVINE_KIRI_JOLITH_TEST.title: THE SWORD'S QUESTION
prose: *The Field — After Combat*

The enemy is routed. Your blade is still hot from use. And standing before you, though no one else can see him, is a figure in battered armor. His face is scarred. His eyes are ancient. He carries a sword that has killed gods.
Kiri-Jolith. The Bison of Heaven. God of righteous warfare.
**'Squire.'** His voice is thunder on a clear day. **'You fight well. But do you know *why* you fight?'**
He doesn't wait for an answer.

**'Not for glory. Not for honour. Not even for the Measure, though the Measure shapes you. You fight because others cannot. Because the strong must shield the weak. Because courage means nothing if it only protects yourself.'**
He raises his blade in salute.

**'When the moment comes — and it will come — you will choose between victory and righteousness. Remember this: the Sword does not serve the knight. The knight serves the Sword. We are instruments of protection, not conquest.'**
Then he is gone. The battlefield is still. Your companions are looting corpses. Life continues. But something fundamental has shifted in how you understand your oath.

boxes.text:
**DIVINE TEST — KIRI-JOLITH**
The God of Just War has marked you. When you use Smite to defend an innocent or protect a companion, add an additional 1d8 radiant damage.

choices.text: I will remember. The sword serves the people, not the other way around.

DEPTH_CAERON_PRIDE.title: THE MASK
prose: *Camp — After Dark*
Lord Caeron sits apart from the others, methodically cleaning his already immaculate armor. The firelight catches the silver filigree — House Ashvale's mark.
'They think I'm arrogant.' He doesn't look up. 'I am. It's easier than the alternative.'
He sets down the cloth.

'My father lost everything in the Cataclysm. Not land — we kept the name, the titles, the hollow prestige. He lost his *certainty*. The gods he prayed to went silent. The order he served became a joke. He spent his last years drunk and raging at a sky that wouldn't answer.'
Caeron's jaw tightens.

'So I built walls. Made sure no one could see the doubt underneath. If I act like I'm better than everyone, maybe I'll start to believe it. Maybe they will too.' He finally meets your eyes. 'It's exhausting. Pretending to be unbreakable when you're held together with pride and spite.'

choices.text: 'The fact that you can admit it — that's real strength. Not the armor.'
'We're all pretending. The Measure is just structured pretending.'
Offer him a drink. Some truths are easier with wine.

DEPTH_SERA_SHADOWS.title: THE DEBT
prose: *Camp — Third Watch*
Sera appears beside you like smoke. You've stopped being startled by it.
'I killed someone once who didn't deserve it.' Her voice is flat, matter-of-fact. 'I was young. Desperate. Someone paid me to make a man disappear. I didn't ask why until after.'
She watches the darkness beyond the firelight.

'Turned out he was a healer. Had a family. The person who hired me was his sister's creditor — wanted the inheritance.' A long pause. 'I found that out three years later. By then I'd killed a dozen people. Some deserved it. Most didn't.'
Her silver-streaked hair catches moonlight.
'I don't sleep well. That's why I take the third watch. It's not generosity — it's penance. Every night I'm awake is a night I'm not dreaming about the faces of people I made into ghosts.'

choices.text: 'The fact that it haunts you means you're not the person who did those things anymore.'
'What happened to the creditor?'
Take the watch with her. Silence can be its own comfort.

DEPTH_VAEL_STANDARD.title: THE RING
prose: *Camp — Dawn*
Vael is doing sword forms in the grey pre-dawn light. Perfect. Relentless. The Solamnic signet on its cord swings with each movement.

She catches you watching and doesn't stop. 'My mother's ring.' Thrust. Parry. 'She wasn't a knight — women couldn't join the Order back then. But she trained like one. Fought like one. Died like one, defending our holdings when the goblin raids came.'
Her blade catches the first sunlight.
'The Order gave her a posthumous commendation. *Posthumous*.' Her voice is bitter. 'They couldn't acknowledge her in life, so they praised her in death. As if that made up for anything.'
She sheaths the blade.

'I wear the ring to remember what she earned and was never given. I train twice as hard because I have to be twice as good to get half the respect.' She finally looks at you. 'That's not complaint. That's fact.'

choices.text: 'You're already better than half the knights I've met. And they know it — that's why they resent you.'
'The Order is changing. Slowly. People like you are why.'
Draw your blade. 'Show me those forms.' Learn from her.

DEPTH_TARIEL_TRUTH.title: THE QUESTION
prose: *Camp — Midnight*
Tariel finds you awake, which seems to please her. She has that look — the one that means she's been thinking.

'I've been wondering.' She sits beside you, close enough that her shoulder almost touches yours. 'Why do you do this? Not the grand reasons — the Oath, the Measure, protecting the innocent. The real reason. The one underneath.'
She waits. Tariel always waits. She knows silence makes people fill it with truth.

'Because I've watched a lot of people answer that question,' she continues eventually. 'The ones who fight for glory burn out first. The ones who fight for vengeance burn everyone around them. The ones who fight because they don't know what else to do — they're the dangerous ones. They stop caring when it matters.'
Her green eyes study your face.
'The ones who last? They fight because they love something more than they fear losing it. I'm trying to figure out what you love.'

choices.text: 'The people beside me. The column behind me. Something that simple.'
'I don't know yet. I'm still figuring it out.'
'What do you fight for, Tariel?'

DEPTH_LYSANDER_HONOUR.title: THE FALL
prose: *Camp — Dusk*
Lysander sits sharpening his battered longsword. There's no ornament on it — there used to be. You can see where the pommel was replaced with plain iron.
'I was a knight once.' He doesn't look up. 'Order of the Sword. Had the tabard, the title, the family proud enough to burst.' The whetstone rasps against steel. 'Then I made a choice.'
He tests the edge against his thumb.
'My commander ordered us to hold a bridge. Not strategically important — just a noble's property. Refugees were trying to cross. He said let them drown. The bridge mattered more than peasants.'
His voice hardens.

'I refused. Drew on him when he tried to enforce the order. Saved the refugees. Lost everything else.' He finally meets your eyes. 'They called it desertion. Stripped my rank. Burned my tabard. My family hasn't spoken to me in eight years.'

'But the thing is — I'd do it again. Every time. Because the Oath isn't about obeying orders. It's about protecting people who can't protect themselves. And if the Order forgets that, then the Order is wrong.'

choices.text: 'You kept the real oath. The one that matters.'
'That commander — is he still alive?'
Say nothing. Just clasp his arm. Warrior to warrior.

MENTOR_HUMA_ECHO.title: THE DRAGONLANCE DREAM
prose: *Dream — The Field of Huma's Rest*
You know this place. Every Solamnic child knows it from the stories. The field where Huma Dragonbane made his last stand against the Queen of Darkness herself.
But in your dream, he's not fighting. He's sitting on a fallen log, cleaning a blade that shimmers with impossible light. When he sees you, he smiles — tired, knowing, kind.
**'Squire.'** His voice is exactly how you imagined it. **'You're scared. Good. Only fools aren't scared.'**
He gestures for you to sit.

**'They'll tell you I was fearless. I wasn't. I was terrified from the first battle to the last. The difference is — I did what needed doing anyway. Fear is not the enemy. *Letting* fear stop you is.'**
He hands you the blade. It's warm. It feels like holding sunlight.

**'You won't carry this lance. That's not your story. But you'll face your own dragon. Everyone does. When you do — remember: courage isn't the absence of fear. It's deciding that something else matters more.'**
You wake. Your hands are still warm.

boxes.text:
**MENTOR VISION — HUMA**
The greatest knight who ever lived has touched your dreams. When you are frightened or shaken, you have advantage on saves to end the condition.

choices.text: I will remember. Fear is not my master.

MENTOR_BRONWYN_LESSON.title: THE SWORDMASTER'S GHOST
prose: *Dream — The Training Yard*
Swordmaster Bronwyn died three years ago. You were there. But in this dream, she's young again — her hair still black, her stance perfect, her eyes sharp as the blade she carries.
**'Stance.'** The word snaps like a whip. **'Again.'**
You find yourself drilling. High guard. Low guard. Middle guard. The forms you've done ten thousand times. Your muscles remember even when your mind is dreaming.

**'Technique fails,'** she says, circling you. **'Strength fails. Speed fails. But discipline? Discipline is the one thing that gets *better* when everything else gets worse. When you're tired, hurt, outnumbered — discipline is what keeps your guard up when your arms want to drop.'**
She stops in front of you.

**'I taught you the forms. But forms are just movements. What I really taught you is this: when it matters, you don't think. You trust your training. The thinking happens before the fight. The doing happens during.'**
She salutes — the old way, before the reforms.
**'You were my best student. Don't waste that.'**

boxes.text:
**MENTOR VISION — BRONWYN**
Your swordmaster's training runs deep. Once per combat, when you miss an attack, you may reroll the attack die (take the new result).

choices.text: I will trust my training. Thank you, Swordmaster.

MENTOR_ALDOUS_WISDOM.title: THE OLD KNIGHT'S COUNSEL
prose: *Dream — The Chapel*
Aldous sits in the pew where he always sat — third from the front, left side, near the Kingfisher banner. The chapel is empty except for him and the light through the stained glass.
**'Sit.'** His voice is gruff. He never was one for ceremony. **'We need to talk about mercy.'**
You sit. Even in dreams, you don't disobey Aldous.
**'The Measure says a lot about honour. About duty. About oaths and obligations and the proper way to hold a fork at formal dinners.'** He snorts. **'What it doesn't say enough about is mercy.'**
He turns to face you. His eyes are the same — kind and fierce in equal measure.

**'Mercy isn't weakness. Any fool can kill. Mercy is strength — the hardest kind. It means looking at someone who wronged you, who hurt you, who maybe even deserves to die... and choosing to stay your hand anyway. Not because you can't kill them. Because you choose not to.'**
He grips your shoulder.
**'The Order used to understand that. Before the Cataclysm made everyone hard. Remember it. When you have power over someone — mercy is the truest measure of who you are.'**

boxes.text:
**MENTOR VISION — ALDOUS**
The old knight's wisdom stays with you. When you show mercy to a defeated enemy, gain +1 honour and restore 1d6 HP from renewed purpose.

choices.text: I will remember. Mercy is the true strength.

FLAME_ALDRIC.title: WHAT WE ARE
prose: *Camp — After Midnight*
The others are asleep. Aldric sits beside you, closer than he needs to. The fire has burned low. Neither of you has moved to feed it.

'I've been thinking,' he says. 'About what happens after. If there is an after.' He stares at the embers. 'I've trained my whole life to be a knight. To serve, to fight, to die if the Measure demands it. I never thought about what I'd want to *live* for.'
He turns to look at you. The firelight catches the scar through his eyebrow.
'Then I met you. And suddenly I had something to lose.' His voice is rough. 'That terrifies me. But not as much as the idea of never saying it.'
He reaches for your hand. His fingers are calloused, warm, shaking slightly.
'Whatever this is between us — I don't want to pretend it isn't there anymore.'

choices.text: Take his hand. Pull him closer. Let the fire burn out.
'I feel it too. But the timing... we're in the middle of a war.'
'Aldric... I care for you. But not like that.'

FLAME_THESSALY.title: THE LOCKET OPENS
prose: *Camp — Before Dawn*
Thessaly finds you awake. She sits beside you without speaking, then holds out her locket. It's open.
Inside is a tiny portrait. A woman with Thessaly's copper hair and sharp eyes, rendered in miniature with extraordinary skill.
'I opened it,' she whispers. 'I thought I'd fall apart. But I didn't. Because you were here. Because somehow, being near you makes the hard things... possible.'
She closes the locket and holds it to her chest.
'I'm not good at this. Feelings. People. I push everyone away because it's safer than letting them see me.' She meets your eyes. 'But I don't want to push you away. Not anymore.'
Her hand finds yours in the darkness.
'Stay. Just... stay.'

choices.text: Stay. Watch the stars wheel overhead. Let the silence say everything.
'I'm not going anywhere. But I need you to know — I'm here as a friend.'
'Thessaly, I can't be what you need. I'm sorry.'

FLAME_MIRA.title: HEALING HANDS
prose: *Medical Tent — Evening*
Mira is treating a wound on your arm. Minor — a scratch from the last skirmish. But she insisted.
Her hands are gentle, sure, warm. She works in silence, her dark eyes focused on the bandage.
'I've healed a lot of people,' she says finally. 'Soldiers, refugees, children. I learned to keep distance. Professional. Safe.' She ties off the bandage but doesn't release your arm.
'I can't keep distance from you.' Her voice catches. 'Every time you ride into battle, I can't breathe until you come back. Every wound you take feels like it's on my own skin.'
She looks up. Her eyes are bright with unshed tears.
'I'm supposed to heal everyone equally. But I can't. Not when it comes to you.'

choices.text: Cup her face in your hands. Kiss her tears away.
'Your care means everything. But I don't want to compromise your healing.'
'Mira, I value you too much to let this become complicated.'

FLAME_SERATH.title: THE COMMANDER'S HEART
prose: *Command Tent — Late Night*
